problems.title: Frankl's Union-Closed Sets Conjecture
problems.tagline: In any union-closed family, some element lies in at least half the sets.
problems.summary: Every finite union-closed set family contains an element in ≥50% of sets.
problems.description: Proposed by Péter Frankl in 1979, the union-closed sets conjecture states that for any finite family of sets F (with |F| ≥ 2) that is closed under pairwise unions, there exists an element contained in at least half of the sets of F. Despite its elementary statement, the conjecture resisted all attacks for over four decades. In November 2022 Justin Gilmer achieved the first breakthrough by introducing entropy/information-theoretic methods to prove a constant lower bound of roughly 0.01. This bound was rapidly pushed to (3 - √5)/2 ≈ 0.38 by Sawin, Chase-Lovett, Alweiss-Huang-Sellke, and others. Closing the remaining gap to 1/2 now appears tractable within a decade, making this one of the most attackable open combinatorial problems.
problems.abstract: We investigate Frankl's union-closed sets conjecture, which asserts that any finite union-closed family of sets contains an element belonging to at least half of its members. Building on Gilmer's 2022 entropy-based breakthrough and subsequent refinements reaching the (3 - √5)/2 threshold, this project aims to close the remaining gap to 1/2 through improved information-theoretic inequalities, structural analysis of extremal families, and hybrid combinatorial-entropic techniques. We will formalize the entropy compression argument, explore tightness via explicit constructions, and test candidate improvements computationally on small families before attempting an analytic proof. Success would resolve a 45-year-old conjecture and establish a template for entropy methods in extremal set theory.

problems.title: Sendov's Conjecture
problems.tagline: Every root of a polynomial has a critical point within distance 1.
problems.summary: For polynomials with roots in the unit disk, each root has a critical point within unit distance.
problems.description: Sendov's conjecture (1958) states that if p(z) is a polynomial of degree n ≥ 2 all of whose zeros lie in the closed unit disk, then for every zero r of p, there exists a zero of p'(z) within distance 1 of r. The conjecture connects root locations and critical points in a surprisingly subtle way. Terence Tao achieved a dramatic advance in 2020 by proving the conjecture for all sufficiently large degrees n using a combination of compactness arguments, Lagrange multipliers, and delicate inequality analysis. The remaining open cases are finite and, in principle, computationally verifiable. Closing them could yield a complete proof within a few years.
problems.abstract: This project targets the full resolution of Sendov's conjecture on critical points of complex polynomials with roots confined to the unit disk. Tao's 2020 proof covers all sufficiently large degrees via compactness and perturbative methods, leaving a finite but nontrivial range of intermediate degrees open. We propose a hybrid strategy: (1) sharpen Tao's effective bound to reduce the open window, (2) deploy rigorous interval arithmetic and semidefinite programming to verify remaining cases computationally, and (3) seek a unified analytic proof that subsumes all degrees. Successful completion would close a 67-year-old problem in complex analysis and polynomial geometry.

problems.title: SIC-POVM Existence (Zauner's Conjecture)
problems.tagline: Symmetric informationally complete measurements exist in every dimension.
problems.summary: Equiangular tight frames of d² vectors exist in every complex dimension d.
problems.description: Zauner's conjecture (1999) posits that in every complex Hilbert space of dimension d, there exists a set of d² unit vectors with pairwise squared inner products equal to 1/(d+1) — a symmetric informationally complete positive operator-valued measure (SIC-POVM). These structures are central to quantum state tomography and foundations of quantum mechanics. Numerical solutions have been found in every dimension up to d = 193, and exact algebraic solutions are known in many dimensions, revealing deep connections to algebraic number theory (Stark units, real quadratic fields, and Hilbert's 12th problem). Recent work by Appleby, Flammia, Kopp and collaborators has transformed this from a quantum-information curiosity into a number-theoretic frontier.
problems.abstract: We propose a systematic investigation of Zauner's SIC-POVM existence conjecture via the algebraic-number-theoretic framework developed by Appleby-Flammia-Kopp. Numerical SIC-POVMs are known in all dimensions up to 193, and emerging theory links their matrix entries to Stark units over real quadratic fields. Our approach combines (1) extension of numerical search to higher dimensions using high-precision arithmetic, (2) systematic construction of exact solutions via ray class fields of ℚ(√(d²-4)), and (3) establishing structural theorems that imply existence in infinite families of dimensions. This would simultaneously resolve a central conjecture in quantum information and provide empirical evidence for Hilbert's 12th problem in the real quadratic case.

problems.title: Cap Set Problem (Generalization)
problems.tagline: How large can a subset of 𝔽₃ⁿ be without three collinear points?
problems.summary: Asymptotic growth rate of three-term progression-free subsets of 𝔽₃ⁿ.
problems.description: The cap set problem asks for the maximum size of a subset of 𝔽₃ⁿ containing no three-term arithmetic progression (equivalently, no three points on an affine line). After decades with only logarithmic improvements over the trivial bound, Croot-Lev-Pach (2016) and Ellenberg-Gijswijt (2016) delivered a stunning breakthrough via the polynomial method, proving that cap sets have size O(2.756ⁿ). The method opened entirely new directions in extremal combinatorics and has generated dozens of follow-up papers. Generalizations to 𝔽pⁿ for larger p, tensor rank questions, and the exact asymptotic constant all remain active.
problems.abstract: Following the Croot-Lev-Pach polynomial method breakthrough, the cap set problem and its generalizations have become a testing ground for new extremal techniques. This project targets (1) sharpening the exponential base in the 𝔽₃ⁿ cap set upper bound toward the conjectured tight value, (2) extending the slice rank method to cap sets in 𝔽pⁿ for p ≥ 5 and establishing quantitative improvements, and (3) connecting cap set bounds to tensor rank lower bounds and matrix multiplication complexity. The goal is a unified framework linking polynomial methods, representation theory, and arithmetic combinatorics, with potential algorithmic consequences for fast matrix multiplication.

problems.title: Lonely Runner Conjecture
problems.tagline: On a circular track, every runner becomes lonely at some moment.
problems.summary: For k runners with distinct integer speeds, each is eventually distance ≥ 1/k from all others.
problems.description: Formulated independently by Wills (1967) and Cusick (1973), the lonely runner conjecture considers k runners starting at the origin of a unit circle moving at distinct constant integer speeds. It asserts that for each runner, there is a moment when they are at distance at least 1/k (along the track) from every other runner — i.e., they feel 'lonely'. Despite its elementary formulation, the conjecture has resisted general proof for over 55 years. It is known for k ≤ 7 through a combination of Diophantine approximation arguments and careful case analysis. The problem is equivalent to a statement about view obstructions in high-dimensional lattices and connects to nowhere-zero flows in graph theory.
problems.abstract: This project pursues resolution of the lonely runner conjecture for k = 8 and beyond, building on the Diophantine approximation framework that has settled cases k ≤ 7. Our strategy combines (1) refined Bohr set arguments to reduce the search space for potential counterexamples, (2) computer-verified case analysis using interval arithmetic over the speed simplex, and (3) new structural results relating the conjecture to nowhere-zero 1/(k-1)-flows on regular matroids. A successful proof for general k would resolve a central question in Diophantine approximation and establish new techniques applicable to view-obstruction problems in geometry of numbers.

problems.title: Rota's Basis Conjecture
problems.tagline: n bases of an n-dimensional space can be rearranged into n transversal bases.
problems.summary: Given n bases of an n-dim vector space, their nm vectors form n disjoint transversal bases.
problems.description: Rota's basis conjecture (circa 1989) states that if B₁, ..., Bₙ are n bases of an n-dimensional vector space (or more generally, n disjoint bases of a matroid of rank n), then it is possible to arrange the nm = n² vectors into an n × n grid such that each row is one of the given bases Bᵢ and each column is also a basis of the space. Substantial progress has been made: the conjecture is known for matroids of rank ≤ 4, for paving matroids, and — in a weakened form — probabilistically for all rank via the recent work of Bucić, Kwan, Pokrovskiy and Sudakov. A full deterministic proof still eludes researchers but now seems within reach.
problems.abstract: This project pursues a complete proof of Rota's basis conjecture, building on the probabilistic near-proofs by Bucić-Kwan-Pokrovskiy-Sudakov and the algebraic techniques of Aharoni-Berger. Our plan is to (1) derandomize the probabilistic arguments via entropy compression, (2) extend structural results from representable to general matroids, and (3) develop a unified framework treating Latin square transversal problems, perfect matchings in matroid intersection, and Rota's conjecture as facets of a single combinatorial principle. A full resolution would cap a 35-year research program and enrich matroid theory with new algorithmic tools.

problems.title: Juggler Sequence Convergence
problems.tagline: Every juggler sequence eventually reaches 1.
problems.summary: Iterating n → ⌊n^{1/2}⌋ (even) or ⌊n^{3/2}⌋ (odd) always terminates at 1.
problems.description: The juggler sequence, introduced by Clifford Pickover, is defined by the iteration: if n is even, send n to ⌊n^{1/2}⌋; if n is odd, send n to ⌊n^{3/2}⌋. The conjecture asserts that starting from any positive integer, this sequence eventually reaches 1. Unlike the Collatz problem, the juggler sequence grows rapidly before collapsing and is structurally simpler: the expected logarithmic step size has a clear sign bias, suggesting probabilistic termination. The conjecture has been verified computationally for enormous starting values, and heuristic arguments based on random walks in log space give strong support. A rigorous proof would likely come from effective ergodic theory or an averaging argument that Collatz itself resists.
problems.abstract: We investigate convergence of the juggler sequence defined by n → ⌊n^{1/2}⌋ (even) or n → ⌊n^{3/2}⌋ (odd), conjectured to terminate at 1 for all positive integer starts. Unlike Collatz, juggler sequences have a positive expected drift in log coordinates, making them amenable to probabilistic analysis. Our strategy combines (1) rigorous random-walk style estimates proving that 'almost all' starting values terminate, (2) effective termination time bounds via Brun-type sieve methods, and (3) structural exclusion of potential non-trivial cycles using Diophantine constraints on perfect-square neighborhoods. Success would yield the first fully resolved Collatz-style iteration problem and offer tools applicable to related hailstone dynamics.

problems.title: Twin Prime Conjecture
problems.tagline: Infinitely many prime pairs differ by exactly 2.
problems.summary: There exist infinitely many primes p such that p + 2 is also prime.
problems.description: The twin prime conjecture asserts that there are infinitely many prime pairs (p, p+2). Dating back at least to de Polignac (1849), it remains one of the oldest and most famous open problems in number theory. Decisive progress came in 2013 when Yitang Zhang proved the existence of infinitely many prime pairs differing by at most 70 million, employing an ingenious variant of the Goldston-Pintz-Yıldırım (GPY) sieve. James Maynard (2013) and the Polymath project subsequently reduced the gap to 246, and under Elliott-Halberstam-type assumptions to 6. The remaining factor-of-three gap to the twin-prime target of 2 appears to require a genuine new idea beyond sieve methods.
problems.abstract: We pursue progress on the twin prime conjecture within the Zhang-Maynard-Tao framework. While bounded gaps have been established at ≤ 246 unconditionally and ≤ 6 under Elliott-Halberstam, closing to a gap of 2 requires genuinely new input. Our approach explores (1) improved level-of-distribution estimates for primes in arithmetic progressions using the q-analogue of van der Corput, (2) multiplicative chaos and random model refinements of the Cramér heuristic, and (3) modular automorphic input via GL(3) L-functions to bypass the Elliott-Halberstam barrier. The project aims to push bounded gaps significantly below 246 and develop tools with implications for Goldbach and Polignac conjectures.

problems.title: Collatz Conjecture (3n+1 Problem)
problems.tagline: Iterating the 3n+1 map always reaches 1.
problems.summary: For any positive integer, iterating n→n/2 (even) or n→3n+1 (odd) reaches 1.
problems.description: The Collatz conjecture, introduced by Lothar Collatz in 1937, iterates the map T(n) = n/2 if n is even and T(n) = 3n+1 if n is odd, conjecturing that every positive integer eventually reaches 1. Despite its elementary statement, Erdős famously remarked that 'mathematics is not yet ready for such problems.' Computational verification extends past 2⁶⁸, and in 2019 Terence Tao proved that 'almost all' orbits eventually enter a bounded region — the strongest general result known. Key obstructions include the lack of an invariant measure, the intricate interplay of multiplicative and additive structures, and the absence of tools to rule out non-trivial cycles in full generality.
problems.abstract: Building on Tao's 2019 'almost all' result for Collatz orbits, this project pursues structural advances toward a full proof. Our strategy combines (1) ergodic-theoretic analysis of the 2-adic extension of the Collatz map seeking an invariant measure on ℤ₂, (2) Diophantine exclusion of large non-trivial cycles via refined linear-forms-in-logarithms bounds, and (3) model-theoretic reformulations connecting Collatz to generalized Baker's theorem. While full resolution may remain elusive, intermediate goals include extending 'almost all' to 'all but finitely many' in various density senses, and sharpening the known cycle length lower bounds.

problems.title: Lehmer's Conjecture on Mahler Measure
problems.tagline: There is a smallest Mahler measure greater than 1 among algebraic integers.
problems.summary: The Mahler measure of a non-cyclotomic algebraic integer is bounded below by a constant > 1.
problems.description: Lehmer's conjecture (1933) asks whether there exists an absolute constant c > 1 such that every algebraic integer α that is not a root of unity has Mahler measure M(α) ≥ c. The current record is held by Lehmer's own polynomial of degree 10 with Mahler measure ≈ 1.17628, which is conjectured to be minimal. Dobrowolski's bound gives M(α) ≥ 1 + c(log log d / log d)³ for degree-d algebraic integers — the best unconditional result. The problem touches transcendence theory, heights of algebraic numbers, and arithmetic dynamics. It is directly connected to ASRP research on Mahler measures and special values of L-functions via the Deninger conjectures.
problems.abstract: We investigate Lehmer's conjecture on the smallest Mahler measure of non-cyclotomic algebraic integers, building on Dobrowolski's bound and ASRP's internal work on Mahler measure identities. Our approach combines (1) exploitation of Boyd-Deninger type formulas linking Mahler measures to special values of L-functions, (2) automorphic techniques applied to the universal Mahler measure, and (3) height-function methods from arithmetic dynamics to constrain potential low-measure examples. A successful proof would establish a fundamental spectral gap in algebraic number theory with consequences for transcendence, heights on elliptic curves, and the BC-system approach to L-functions.

problems.title: Erdős–Straus Conjecture
problems.tagline: 4/n is always a sum of three unit fractions.
problems.summary: For every n ≥ 2, the equation 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z has a positive integer solution.
problems.description: The Erdős–Straus conjecture (1948) asserts that for every integer n ≥ 2, the equation 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z has a solution in positive integers x, y, z. Computational verification extends to n ≤ 10¹⁷, and modular arithmetic arguments cover all residues mod 840 except for primes in a small set of congruence classes. The remaining open cases form a thin set but cannot be eliminated by any known unified argument. The conjecture is a special case of broader questions about Egyptian fraction representations and unit-fraction densities.
problems.abstract: This project targets the Erdős–Straus conjecture 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z through a two-pronged strategy. First, we extend the residue-class covering approach by systematically analyzing congruence obstructions beyond mod 840, potentially using the Selberg-Delange method to show density results. Second, we pursue an analytic proof via character sum estimates and the circle method, treating the number of representations as a Fourier coefficient. Complementarily, we will push the computational verification frontier past 10¹⁸ using GPU-accelerated modular search. Resolution would close a 77-year-old conjecture and illuminate general Egyptian fraction density problems.

problems.title: Artin's Conjecture on Primitive Roots
problems.tagline: Every non-square integer ≠ -1 is a primitive root for infinitely many primes.
problems.summary: For a ≠ -1 and non-square, a is a primitive root mod p for infinitely many primes p.
problems.description: Artin's conjecture (1927) posits that for any integer a that is neither -1 nor a perfect square, there are infinitely many primes p for which a is a primitive root modulo p (i.e., generates the full multiplicative group (ℤ/pℤ)*). Artin further conjectured the density of such primes, expressible as a rational multiple of Artin's constant C = ∏(1 - 1/(p(p-1))) ≈ 0.3739. Hooley proved the full conjecture in 1967 conditional on the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis for Dedekind zeta functions of Kummer extensions. Heath-Brown (1986) proved that at most two primes can fail to be primitive roots for infinitely many primes — an unconditional result that falls just short of the full conjecture.
problems.abstract: We investigate Artin's conjecture on primitive roots, currently known only conditionally on GRH via Hooley's 1967 proof. Our approach seeks unconditional progress through (1) sharper sieve methods applied to the indicator function of primitive roots, refining Heath-Brown's at-most-two-exceptions result, (2) bilinear forms estimates for character sums over Kummer extensions to bypass GRH assumptions, and (3) connection with the Chebotarev density theorem under weakened hypotheses. A successful unconditional proof would be a milestone in analytic number theory and provide a template for attacking other problems currently dependent on GRH.

problems.title: Uniform Boundedness of Rational Points
problems.tagline: Genus-g curves have boundedly many rational points over number fields.
problems.summary: The number of rational points on a curve of genus ≥ 2 is bounded uniformly in terms of g and the number field.
problems.description: Following Faltings' proof of Mordell's conjecture (1983) — every curve of genus ≥ 2 over a number field has finitely many rational points — a natural strengthening asks whether the number of such points can be uniformly bounded by a function of the genus and the number field alone, independent of the curve. Caporaso, Harris, and Mazur (1997) showed that this uniform boundedness follows from Lang's conjecture on rational points of varieties of general type. Significant partial results exist, and the problem links to height bounds, Vojta's conjectures, and the Bombieri-Lang conjecture.
problems.abstract: This project pursues uniform boundedness for rational points on curves of genus g ≥ 2 over a fixed number field K. Building on Caporaso-Harris-Mazur's reduction to Lang's conjecture and recent progress on Vojta's conjectures, our strategy combines (1) effective Mordell using the Chabauty-Kim nonabelian method, aiming for explicit bounds depending only on g and K, (2) uniform Manin-Mumford style results for Jacobians, and (3) height-machine refinements via arithmetic intersection theory on compactified moduli. Success would yield a dramatic structural insight into Diophantine geometry and could provide the first uniform effective bounds across families of curves.

problems.title: Kepler Conjecture in Higher Dimensions
problems.tagline: Which lattices give densest sphere packings in dimensions other than 1,2,3,8,24?
problems.summary: Densest sphere packings are unknown beyond dimensions 1, 2, 3, 8, and 24.
problems.description: The sphere packing problem asks for the densest arrangement of non-overlapping congruent spheres in ℝⁿ. Hales resolved the classical Kepler conjecture for n = 3 in 1998 (formal verification 2014). A spectacular breakthrough came from Maryna Viazovska, who solved n = 8 in 2016 using modular forms and magic functions, earning the 2022 Fields Medal. Her method, extended by Cohn-Kumar-Miller-Radchenko-Viazovska, also solved n = 24. All other dimensions remain open. The n = 4 case, where the D₄ lattice is conjectured optimal, is particularly tantalizing. The problem is directly connected to ASRP research on cusp form dimensions and modular-form-based energy minimization.
problems.abstract: This project pursues sphere packing upper bounds in dimensions beyond the solved cases n = 1, 2, 3, 8, 24. Building on Viazovska's modular-form 'magic function' approach, we aim to (1) construct candidate Cohn-Elkies linear programming bounds in n = 4, 12, 16, 32 using computer search over cusp form spaces, (2) exploit ASRP's internal results on dimensions of cusp form spaces to constrain the search, and (3) develop analogous methods for energy minimization. The immediate target is the n = 4 case with the conjectured D₄ optimum. Success would extend humanity's catalog of solved sphere packing dimensions beyond the highly exceptional E₈ and Leech cases.

problems.title: Brennan's Conjecture
problems.tagline: Conformal maps of simply connected domains have bounded derivative integrability.
problems.summary: For conformal f: Ω → 𝔻, ∫|f'|^p dA < ∞ for 4/3 < p < 4.
problems.description: Brennan's conjecture (1978) states that for any conformal mapping f from a simply connected planar domain Ω onto the unit disk 𝔻, the integral ∫_Ω |f'(z)|^p dA(z) is finite for all 4/3 < p < 4. The endpoint p = 4 is sharp by the Koebe function example, and the lower bound 4/3 is the Brennan prediction. The conjecture has been verified for narrower ranges — currently (4/3 - ε, 4) for some ε > 0 from work of Pommerenke, Bertilsson, and Hedenmalm-Kayumov-Melentijević. The problem is equivalent to a statement about integrability of derivatives of univalent functions and connects to the analytic theory of Schramm-Loewner evolution.
problems.abstract: This project addresses Brennan's conjecture on integral means of conformal maps. The current best unconditional lower bound leaves a gap to the conjectured threshold p = 4/3. Our approach combines (1) Bertilsson-type extremal problems to reduce to a variational inequality, (2) Loewner-equation analysis using SLE techniques to track the evolution of integral means, and (3) numerical identification of candidate extremal domains with rigorous certificate-based bounds. The eventual goal is a complete proof of integrability on the full interval (4/3, 4), which would resolve a 47-year-old central conjecture in geometric function theory.

problems.title: Crouzeix's Conjecture
problems.tagline: The numerical range controls the norm of polynomials of matrices with constant 2.
problems.summary: For any matrix A and polynomial p, ||p(A)|| ≤ 2 · sup_{z ∈ W(A)} |p(z)|.
problems.description: Crouzeix's conjecture (2004) asserts that for any square matrix A and any polynomial p, ||p(A)|| ≤ 2 · max_{z ∈ W(A)} |p(z)|, where W(A) is the numerical range (field of values) of A and || · || is the operator 2-norm. Crouzeix himself proved the inequality with constant 1 + √2 ≈ 2.414 in 2016. The sharp constant 2 is known to be optimal if it holds. The conjecture has immediate implications for the convergence analysis of Krylov subspace iterative methods and for polynomial numerical hull theory. A resolution appears tantalizingly close.
problems.abstract: We investigate Crouzeix's conjecture, the assertion ||p(A)|| ≤ 2 max_{W(A)} |p(z)|, currently known with constant 1 + √2 via Crouzeix-Palencia. Our approach includes (1) refinement of the Riesz-Dunford functional calculus argument to close the gap from 1 + √2 to 2, (2) extremal analysis for low-rank matrices where the constant is known sharp, and (3) semidefinite programming certificates for the conjecture on specific matrix families. The work has direct relevance to iterative linear solvers, spectral theory, and matrix analysis, and successful resolution would settle a central conjecture in operator theory.

problems.title: 1/3-2/3 Conjecture
problems.tagline: Every finite poset has a pair of elements with balanced comparison probability.
problems.summary: In any finite non-linear poset, some pair (x,y) has Pr[x<y] ∈ [1/3, 2/3] over random linear extensions.
problems.description: The 1/3-2/3 conjecture (Fredman, Linial, independently others) asserts that in any finite partially ordered set that is not a total order, there exists a pair of elements x, y such that the probability Pr(x < y) over uniformly random linear extensions lies in the interval [1/3, 2/3]. This would imply a tight balanced-pair lower bound for sorting algorithms on posets. Kahn and Saks (1984) proved the weaker bound [3/11, 8/11] using a correlation inequality. Further small improvements have been made, but the gap to the conjectured 1/3 has not been fully closed. Recent entropy-compression and entropy-method approaches offer promise.
problems.abstract: We pursue the 1/3-2/3 balanced pair conjecture via entropy-method refinements. Starting from the Kahn-Saks 3/11 bound and subsequent improvements, our strategy combines (1) log-concavity inequalities for linear extension counts via Stanley's theorem, (2) entropy-compression techniques inspired by Gilmer's Frankl-conjecture approach, and (3) probabilistic analysis on the Bruhat-order random walk. The project has direct algorithmic implications for sorting under partial order oracles, potentially yielding improved worst-case bounds for poset sorting.

problems.title: Erdős-Rado Sunflower Conjecture
problems.tagline: Large uniform set systems contain large sunflowers.
problems.summary: Every family of k-sets of size > c^k contains a sunflower (Δ-system) of r petals.
problems.description: The Erdős-Rado sunflower conjecture (1960) asserts that for every integer r ≥ 2, there exists a constant c = c(r) such that any family of k-element sets containing more than c^k sets must contain a sunflower with r petals (r sets whose pairwise intersections are all equal to a common 'core'). Erdős-Rado proved the bound ((r-1))^k · k!. In 2019, Alweiss, Lovett, Wu, and Zhang achieved a breakthrough, reducing the bound to (log k)^{k(1+o(1))} — a near-exponential improvement. The conjectured c^k bound still stands, but the path now looks clear.
problems.abstract: Building on the Alweiss-Lovett-Wu-Zhang 2019 sunflower lemma (bound (log k)^{k(1+o(1))}), this project targets the full Erdős-Rado conjecture with exponential bound c^k. Our approach combines (1) further sharpening the encoding argument via robust entropy compression, (2) investigating additive structure in extremal set systems where bounds are tightest, and (3) developing applications to Boolean circuit lower bounds and constraint satisfaction. A complete resolution of the sunflower conjecture would be a landmark in extremal combinatorics with broad consequences throughout theoretical computer science.

problems.title: Closed Formula for Dedekind Numbers
problems.tagline: Count monotone Boolean functions on n variables.
problems.summary: Find a closed-form expression (or asymptotic) for Dedekind's M(n).
problems.description: Dedekind numbers M(n) count the number of monotone Boolean functions on n variables (equivalently, antichains in the Boolean lattice 2^{[n]}). They grow doubly exponentially. The value M(9) was computed only in 2023 (two independent groups using massive parallel computation), extending the known sequence to nine terms. No closed-form formula is known, and asymptotic estimates are weak. The problem connects to the counting of antichains, ideal structures, and — via Kleitman's theorem — to correlation inequalities. Finding a closed form or a sharper asymptotic remains a flagship combinatorial challenge.
problems.abstract: We investigate the Dedekind number sequence M(n), counting monotone Boolean functions on n variables. Following the 2023 computation of M(9), our project aims at (1) a push to M(10) via improved symmetry reduction and distributed computing, (2) sharper asymptotic estimates of log M(n) using the Korshunov method and refined entropy bounds, and (3) search for algebraic or generating-function identities that could yield a closed form. Connections to Boolean function complexity, threshold phenomena, and ideal counting make this a natural hub problem whose advances have wide combinatorial implications.

problems.title: MLC — Mandelbrot Set Local Connectivity
problems.tagline: The Mandelbrot set is locally connected.
problems.summary: Local connectivity of M would imply density of hyperbolicity for quadratic maps.
problems.description: The MLC conjecture asserts that the Mandelbrot set M is locally connected as a topological space. Douady and Hubbard showed that MLC implies the density of hyperbolicity in the family z ↦ z² + c — one of the central conjectures in complex dynamics. Substantial partial results exist: Yoccoz proved MLC at all finitely renormalizable parameters, and further progress through the work of Kahn, Lyubich, and others covers broad classes of infinitely renormalizable parameters. The remaining cases involve deep bounded-geometry estimates and complex analytic techniques specific to quadratic-like maps.
problems.abstract: This project targets the Mandelbrot-set local connectivity conjecture (MLC) via the Yoccoz-Kahn-Lyubich puzzle-and-para-puzzle framework. We focus on (1) extending bounded-geometry theorems to the remaining classes of infinitely renormalizable parameters, particularly Feigenbaum-type accumulation points, (2) rigidity theorems for combinatorially equivalent polynomials, and (3) new transfer-operator methods from renormalization theory. Resolution of MLC would immediately establish density of hyperbolicity for real quadratic polynomials, settling Fatou's 1920 conjecture and a Smale problem for the 21st century.

problems.title: Fatou Conjecture (Density of Hyperbolicity)
problems.tagline: Hyperbolic rational maps are dense in parameter space.
problems.summary: For every rational map f, hyperbolic maps can be found arbitrarily close to f.
problems.description: Fatou's 1920 conjecture asserts that hyperbolic rational maps form a dense subset of the space of all rational maps of a fixed degree. The conjecture is equivalent to MLC in the quadratic case (Douady-Hubbard), and was listed by Smale as one of the 'mathematical problems for the next century.' Partial results cover real quadratic families (Lyubich, Graczyk-Świątek) and various subfamilies. The full conjecture remains open for general rational maps of degree ≥ 2, and may be resolved together with MLC.
problems.abstract: We investigate Fatou's density of hyperbolicity conjecture, connecting it to MLC and the structure of the Mandelbrot set's boundary. Our approach combines (1) quasiconformal surgery and holomorphic motions to transfer rigidity results from the quadratic family to higher-degree families, (2) entropy-rigidity arguments via Margulis-type theorems for rational maps, and (3) Teichmüller-theoretic analysis of deformation spaces. Progress on Fatou implies density of hyperbolicity — the cornerstone of complex dynamics — and would resolve one of Smale's 21st-century problems.

problems.title: Connes Embedding Problem
problems.tagline: Every II₁ factor embeds into an ultrapower of the hyperfinite factor.
problems.summary: II₁ factors need not embed in R^ω (disproved 2020 via MIP*=RE).
problems.description: The Connes embedding problem (1976) asked whether every separable II₁ factor M embeds into an ultrapower R^ω of the hyperfinite II₁ factor R. For four decades it was one of the most influential open problems in operator algebras, equivalent (via work of Kirchberg, Ozawa, and Tsirelson) to statements in quantum information theory and game theory. In 2020, Ji, Natarajan, Vidick, Wright, and Yuen announced MIP* = RE, which implies the Connes embedding conjecture is FALSE. The aftermath has opened a rich follow-up landscape: identifying explicit non-embeddable II₁ factors, understanding which factors do embed, and the precise strength of MIP*.
problems.abstract: Following the 2020 disproof of the Connes embedding conjecture via MIP* = RE, a rich follow-up agenda has emerged. This project pursues (1) construction of explicit, natural II₁ factors that fail the Connes embedding property, (2) refinement of the computational-complexity reduction to identify tighter equivalences between operator-algebraic and quantum-complexity classes, and (3) exploration of the BC-system (Bost-Connes) implications, particularly ASRP's hypothesized link between the Connes embedding property and L-function zero distribution. The work connects operator algebras, quantum information, and arithmetic geometry.

problems.title: Goldbach's Conjecture
problems.tagline: Every even number > 2 is a sum of two primes.
problems.summary: The binary Goldbach conjecture: every even integer n ≥ 4 is p + q for primes p, q.
problems.description: Goldbach's conjecture (1742, in a letter to Euler) asserts that every even integer greater than 2 can be expressed as the sum of two primes. It has been verified computationally to 4 × 10¹⁸. Chen's theorem (1973) established that every sufficiently large even integer is the sum of a prime and a product of at most two primes. Vinogradov's three-primes theorem (1937) proved the weak Goldbach conjecture (every odd integer > 5 is a sum of three primes) unconditionally for sufficiently large n, completed by Helfgott in 2013. Binary Goldbach has resisted all approaches since 1973 — sieve methods plateau at '1+2', and genuinely new ideas seem required.
problems.abstract: This project pursues new directions on binary Goldbach, unsolved since Chen's 1973 '1+2' result. Our approach explores (1) moment-method analysis of the Goldbach representation function in short intervals via sieve-plus-character-sum hybrids, (2) automorphic input from GL(2) and GL(3) L-functions exploiting averaged subconvexity, and (3) potential connections to the BC-system framework for prime additive structure. While a full proof likely requires a generational advance, intermediate targets include exceptional set bounds, sharper 'almost all' results for Goldbach, and refinement of Chen's theorem.

problems.title: Odd Perfect Numbers
problems.tagline: Does any odd perfect number exist?
problems.summary: Existence of an odd N with σ(N) = 2N is open; lower bound now exceeds 10^1500.
problems.description: A perfect number is a positive integer equal to the sum of its proper divisors. The smallest perfect number is 6; Euclid characterized all even perfect numbers via Mersenne primes, but no odd perfect number has ever been found. Euler proved any odd perfect number must be of the form p^a · m² with p ≡ a ≡ 1 (mod 4). Modern lower bounds exceed 10^1500, and the number of distinct prime factors must exceed 10. Despite millennia of investigation, nobody knows whether any exists. This is arguably the oldest unsolved problem in mathematics.
problems.abstract: We investigate the existence question for odd perfect numbers, among the oldest open problems in mathematics. Current lower bounds exceed 10^1500 and constrain Ω(N) ≥ 10. Our strategy: (1) extend factor-chain elimination via improved component-factor bounds using Lehmer-type constraints, (2) develop a density-based probabilistic impossibility argument via Markov-chain analysis on divisor sums, and (3) leverage elliptic curve techniques to constrain the 'Eulerian' prime component. While a full non-existence proof remains elusive, intermediate progress pushing bounds deeper would narrow the window significantly.

problems.title: Legendre's Conjecture
problems.tagline: There is always a prime between n² and (n+1)².
problems.summary: For every positive integer n, there exists a prime p with n² < p < (n+1)².
problems.description: Legendre's conjecture asserts that for every positive integer n, there is at least one prime number between n² and (n+1)². This is much stronger than Bertrand's postulate (which gives a prime between n and 2n). Unconditionally, the best known results give a prime in [n, n + n^{0.525}] (Baker-Harman-Pintz), which falls short of the [n², (n+1)²] interval of length 2n+1 ≈ 2√N for N = n². The conjecture is implied by but strictly weaker than the Riemann Hypothesis (via Cramér-style arguments), yet even RH gives gaps ~√N log² N, not √N. Legendre's conjecture thus requires input beyond RH.
problems.abstract: We investigate Legendre's conjecture on primes in short intervals near perfect squares. Currently the best result — a prime in [n, n + n^{0.525}] — falls short of the required gap ~ 2√N. Our approach: (1) exploit Dirichlet L-function zero-density improvements in vertical strips to sharpen the Huxley-Baker-Harman-Pintz framework, (2) apply moment estimates from random matrix theory predictions for ζ(s), and (3) study averaged Legendre-type statements. While full resolution likely requires tools beyond GRH, intermediate targets include proving primes in intervals [n², n² + C·n log^A n] for specific small constants.

problems.title: Generalized Riemann Hypothesis
problems.tagline: All non-trivial zeros of Dirichlet L-functions lie on Re(s) = 1/2.
problems.summary: The Riemann Hypothesis extended to all Dirichlet L-functions (and beyond).
problems.description: The Generalized Riemann Hypothesis (GRH) extends the Riemann Hypothesis from ζ(s) to all Dirichlet L-functions L(s, χ) — and, in its broadest formulation, to all automorphic L-functions. GRH has vast consequences throughout analytic number theory: effective Chebotarev density, conditional proofs of Artin's primitive root conjecture (Hooley), quadratic class number bounds, and many more. It is strictly harder than RH itself, and its resolution would transform our understanding of L-functions. ASRP's BC-system approach on ℚ(√5) is directly relevant as it targets the structure of L-function zeros from an operator-algebraic angle.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis, extending RH to Dirichlet L-functions and automorphic L-functions. While a full proof remains out of reach, our agenda includes (1) zero-density improvements for Dirichlet L-functions in families, (2) applying ASRP's BC-system framework on ℚ(√5) to detect structural constraints on L-function zeros via operator-algebraic spectral analysis, and (3) automorphic-L-function moments and subconvexity. Incremental progress has wide-ranging consequences for analytic number theory, arithmetic geometry, and cryptographic assumptions depending on GRH.

problems.title: Birch-Tate Conjecture
problems.tagline: K₂ of a number field ring controls ζ_K(-1).
problems.summary: For a totally real number field K, #K₂(O_K) relates to ζ_K(-1) via an explicit formula.
problems.description: The Birch-Tate conjecture (1971) relates the order of the algebraic K-theory group K₂(O_K) of the ring of integers of a totally real number field K to the special value ζ_K(-1) of the Dedekind zeta function. Specifically: w₂(K) · |ζ_K(-1)| = #K₂(O_K), where w₂(K) is the largest integer n such that Gal(K(μₙ)/K) has exponent dividing 2. Via work of Wiles, Mazur, and the Main Conjecture of Iwasawa theory, the conjecture is known up to 2-torsion for abelian totally real fields. For non-abelian or mixed cases much remains open. Closely connected to ASRP's research on ζ_K special values such as ζ_K(-3) = 1/60 for K = ℚ(√5).
problems.abstract: We investigate the Birch-Tate conjecture linking #K₂(O_K) and ζ_K(-1) for totally real number fields, focusing on cases beyond the abelian range settled by the Iwasawa Main Conjecture. Our approach combines (1) extension of Wiles-Mazur methods to specific non-abelian Galois extensions, (2) explicit computations for ℚ(√5) and its extensions leveraging ASRP's internal formulas, and (3) connection with the motivic formulation via Beilinson regulators. Progress bridges K-theory, Iwasawa theory, and L-function special values, with direct relevance to ASRP's Golden Chain research program.

problems.title: Inverse Galois Problem
problems.tagline: Is every finite group the Galois group of some Galois extension of ℚ?
problems.summary: Realizing every finite group as Gal(K/ℚ) for some K.
problems.description: The Inverse Galois Problem asks whether every finite group G can be realized as the Galois group of a Galois extension K/ℚ. The problem is solved for all solvable groups (Shafarevich 1954) and for many simple groups via rigidity and the theory of covers of ℙ¹ (Belyi, Malle-Matzat, and others). All 26 sporadic simple groups have been realized, with the notable exception of the Mathieu group M₂₃ (though even this is now close to settled). Non-solvable extensions with prescribed Galois group remain subtle, and the problem is open in general. ASRP's work on Galois symmetry in the BC system gives a representation-theoretic angle.
problems.abstract: This project tackles the Inverse Galois Problem for remaining difficult cases, including non-rigid simple groups and their covers. Our strategy combines (1) rigidity-theoretic construction of covers of ℙ¹ with prescribed ramification and monodromy, (2) moduli-space descent via the field-of-definition problem for Hurwitz spaces, and (3) ASRP's BC-system Galois representations to probe automorphic realizations of Galois groups. Intermediate targets include the realization of specific problem cases (M₂₃ variants) and systematic progress on non-solvable Galois representations arising from ASRP modular-form computations.

problems.title: Goncharov's Conjecture on Polylogarithms
problems.tagline: Motivic cohomology is generated by polylogarithms.
problems.summary: Weight-n motivic cohomology of a field is generated by classical n-polylogarithms.
problems.description: Goncharov's conjecture proposes that the motivic cohomology of a field F (in weight n) is generated in a precise sense by the classical n-polylogarithm functions Liₙ. This would provide an explicit 'motivic calculator' for special values of L-functions via Beilinson's conjectures. The n = 2 case corresponds to the Bloch-Wigner dilogarithm and is essentially understood. Higher weights require understanding motivic complexes, mixed Tate motives, and the structure of algebraic K-theory. Connections to ASRP research via polylogarithms and modular forms are direct.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Goncharov's conjecture that motivic cohomology in weight n is generated by classical n-polylogarithms. Our approach combines (1) explicit motivic-complex computations for n = 3, 4 on specific number fields including ℚ(√5), (2) Beilinson regulator computations linking polylogarithms to L-function special values, and (3) ASRP-relevant modular constructions exploiting the connection between iterated integrals and cusp forms. Progress provides both conceptual advances in mixed Tate motive theory and explicit tools for computing L-value motivic cohomology classes.

problems.title: Zilber-Pink Conjecture
problems.tagline: Unlikely intersections in Shimura varieties are controlled.
problems.summary: Special subvarieties account for all atypical intersections in mixed Shimura varieties.
problems.description: The Zilber-Pink conjecture unifies and generalizes the André-Oort, Mordell-Lang, and Manin-Mumford conjectures into a single statement about atypical (unlikely) intersections of subvarieties in mixed Shimura varieties. Pila-Tsimerman proved André-Oort for A_g (moduli of abelian varieties) using the Pila-Wilkie o-minimality theorem. Generalizations to mixed Shimura varieties and to the Zilber-Pink formulation are ongoing. The conjecture is central to modern arithmetic geometry and connects directly to ASRP's modular-form / Shimura-variety research.
problems.abstract: This project pursues cases of the Zilber-Pink conjecture extending beyond the Pila-Tsimerman resolution of André-Oort for A_g. Our strategy combines (1) refined o-minimality and Pila-Wilkie counting for mixed Shimura varieties, (2) Galois-representation methods for Heegner-type special points, and (3) connection to modular-form techniques in ASRP's research program. Intermediate goals include settling atypical-intersection problems for products of modular curves and extending to Shimura varieties of Hodge type. The work lies at the interface of arithmetic geometry, logic, and modular forms.

problems.title: Cramér's Conjecture on Prime Gaps
problems.tagline: Prime gaps are at most O((log p)²).
problems.summary: pₙ₊₁ - pₙ = O((log pₙ)²).
problems.description: Cramér's conjecture (1936), based on a probabilistic model treating primes as independent Bernoulli variables with density 1/log n, asserts that the gap g_n = p_{n+1} - p_n satisfies g_n = O((log p_n)²). Unconditionally, the best known bound is g_n ≪ p_n^{0.525} (Baker-Harman-Pintz). Under the Riemann Hypothesis one obtains g_n ≪ √(p_n) log p_n, still polynomial in √N. Numerical evidence strongly supports the Cramér bound, with the maximum ratio g_n / (log p_n)² hovering around 0.9. Full proof requires tools far beyond RH.
problems.abstract: This project pursues improvements on unconditional prime gap bounds toward Cramér's conjectured O((log p)²) asymptotic. Our approach combines (1) sharpened zero-density estimates for ζ(s) via moment techniques, (2) pseudorandomness models extending the Maier matrix method, and (3) explicit verification of Cramér-ratio records to refine conjectural constants. While full resolution requires dramatically new tools, intermediate improvements in the Baker-Harman-Pintz exponent would be landmark results in analytic number theory.

problems.title: Schanuel's Conjecture
problems.tagline: Complex exponentials of linearly independent algebraic numbers are algebraically independent.
problems.summary: For lin. indep. z₁,...,zₙ ∈ ℂ, trdeg_ℚ ℚ(z₁,...,zₙ,e^{z₁},...,e^{zₙ}) ≥ n.
problems.description: Schanuel's conjecture (1960s) asserts that if z₁, ..., zₙ are complex numbers linearly independent over ℚ, then the transcendence degree of ℚ(z₁, ..., zₙ, e^{z₁}, ..., e^{zₙ}) over ℚ is at least n. It implies the algebraic independence of e and π, the Lindemann-Weierstrass theorem, Baker's theorem, and a host of other transcendence results. Essentially every known transcendence theorem is a special case or consequence. The conjecture is central to transcendence theory and connects to o-minimality via Wilkie's theorem. Directly relevant to ASRP's work on relations between fundamental constants.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Schanuel's conjecture as the unifying principle of transcendence theory. Our approach combines (1) extension of Wüstholz analytic subgroup methods to push known special cases, (2) model-theoretic analysis via Zilber's exponential field conjectures and o-minimal techniques, and (3) exploration of the ASRP-relevant consequences for fundamental constants (e.g., algebraic relations involving e, π, and the golden-chain constants). While full resolution is likely decades away, intermediate progress produces new transcendence results with immediate applications.

problems.title: Jacobian Conjecture
problems.tagline: A polynomial map with constant Jacobian is invertible.
problems.summary: If F: ℂⁿ → ℂⁿ is polynomial with det(JF) ∈ ℂ*, then F has a polynomial inverse.
problems.description: The Jacobian conjecture (Keller, 1939) asserts that a polynomial map F: ℂⁿ → ℂⁿ whose Jacobian determinant is a nonzero constant must be a polynomial automorphism of ℂⁿ. Despite its elementary statement, the problem has resisted proof for over 85 years and has accumulated dozens of equivalent reformulations (Dixmier conjecture, Poisson automorphism conjecture, etc.). Known for n = 1 (trivial) and many partial results for n = 2, but completely open in general. Many announced 'proofs' have been retracted. The problem is deeply connected to the structure of the Weyl algebra and noncommutative algebra.
problems.abstract: This project pursues the Jacobian conjecture through both analytic and algebraic angles. Our approach combines (1) the Dixmier-conjecture equivalence allowing attack via the first Weyl algebra, (2) Mathieu subspace and Mathieu conjecture reformulations, and (3) explicit computer-verified cases for low-degree polynomial maps using Gröbner basis elimination. A fundamental challenge is avoiding pitfalls that have invalidated prior attempts. Even partial progress in n = 2 with strengthened techniques would be significant.

problems.title: Hadamard Matrix Conjecture
problems.tagline: Hadamard matrices exist in every order divisible by 4.
problems.summary: For every n ≡ 0 (mod 4), an n × n matrix of ±1 with orthogonal rows exists.
problems.description: A Hadamard matrix is an n × n matrix with entries ±1 whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Such matrices can exist only for n = 1, 2, or n ≡ 0 (mod 4). The Hadamard conjecture asserts that a Hadamard matrix exists for every multiple of 4. Known constructions (Paley, Sylvester, Williamson, Turyn) cover most orders, but sporadic gaps remain. The smallest open case was long n = 668 (settled 2005), then n = 764 (open until 2023), and now n = 668 has fallen with others, but the general conjecture is still unproven. The problem connects combinatorial design theory, finite geometry, and number theory.
problems.abstract: This project pursues the Hadamard conjecture through construction and verification of matrices in remaining open orders. Our strategy combines (1) Paley-type constructions leveraging new number-theoretic inputs, (2) computer search via metaheuristics (simulated annealing, evolutionary algorithms) for sporadic orders, and (3) Turyn-Williamson array theory generalizations. Beyond construction, we aim to identify new infinite-family construction principles that could reduce the problem to asymptotic coverage. The work bridges combinatorial design theory, coding theory, and number theory.

problems.title: Köthe's Conjecture
problems.tagline: The sum of two nil ideals is nil.
problems.summary: In any associative ring, the sum of two nil left ideals is a nil ideal.
problems.description: Köthe's conjecture (1930) asserts that in any associative ring R, the sum of two nil left ideals is a nil ideal. Equivalently: every ring R contains a largest nil ideal (the upper nil radical) which equals the lower nil radical. The problem is open after 95 years despite its elementary formulation. Many equivalent formulations have been discovered, and specific classes of rings (Noetherian, PI-rings) satisfy the conjecture, but the general case remains elusive. This is a flagship unsolved problem in noncommutative ring theory.
problems.abstract: We investigate Köthe's conjecture on nil ideals in associative rings, open for nearly a century. Our approach combines (1) analysis of specific candidate counterexample rings via free algebra quotients, (2) connection to the Amitsur problem on the Jacobson radical of polynomial rings, and (3) homological techniques probing the boundary between nil and nilpotent behavior. While a full resolution remains elusive, intermediate targets include sharpening equivalences between known formulations and identifying new classes of rings where the conjecture holds.

problems.title: Smooth 4D Poincaré Conjecture
problems.tagline: Every smooth homotopy 4-sphere is diffeomorphic to S⁴.
problems.summary: The smooth Poincaré conjecture for n = 4, the only dimension where it remains open.
problems.description: The smooth Poincaré conjecture in dimension n asserts that every smooth closed n-manifold homotopy equivalent to the n-sphere is diffeomorphic to S^n. It is proved in dimensions ≥ 5 (Smale, Kervaire-Milnor caveats aside) and famously in n = 3 via Perelman's Ricci flow proof. The n = 4 case is the sole remaining open dimension, and it is special: the smooth category in 4D exhibits phenomena seen nowhere else (exotic ℝ⁴s, exotic structures on closed manifolds). The conjecture could be either true (no exotic 4-spheres) or false (exotic 4-spheres exist). Serious candidates for counterexamples have been proposed and refuted repeatedly.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the smooth 4D Poincaré conjecture — the only dimension where the Poincaré conjecture remains unresolved. Our approach combines (1) analysis of Gluck-twist constructions and Cappell-Shaneson spheres to either construct a counterexample or prove them standard, (2) gauge theory / Seiberg-Witten invariant obstructions applied to candidate exotic 4-spheres, and (3) Khovanov-Rozansky and Floer-homology approaches via Heegaard triples. Resolution requires deep new geometric ideas unique to 4D.

problems.title: Weinstein Conjecture
problems.tagline: Every Reeb vector field on a contact manifold has a closed orbit.
problems.summary: On any closed contact (2n+1)-manifold, the Reeb flow admits a periodic orbit.
problems.description: The Weinstein conjecture (1979) asserts that on any closed contact manifold, the Reeb vector field has at least one closed orbit. In dimension 3, the full conjecture was proved by Taubes (2007) using Seiberg-Witten Floer homology. In higher dimensions the conjecture is open in general — only partial results exist (e.g., for fillable contact manifolds via symplectic field theory, or for specific topological types). The problem lies at the intersection of symplectic topology, dynamical systems, and Floer theory.
problems.abstract: This project targets the Weinstein conjecture in dimensions ≥ 5, building on Taubes' 2007 proof in dimension 3. Our approach combines (1) development of contact homology as a computable Floer-type invariant in higher dimensions, (2) holomorphic curves and symplectic field theory with refined compactness arguments, and (3) extension of Taubes-type Seiberg-Witten techniques to higher dimensions. Intermediate goals include new classes of contact manifolds satisfying Weinstein and development of obstructions that would distinguish potential counterexamples.

problems.title: Andrews-Curtis Conjecture
problems.tagline: Balanced presentations of the trivial group are AC-trivializable.
problems.summary: Any balanced presentation of the trivial group can be reduced via AC moves.
problems.description: The Andrews-Curtis conjecture (1965) asserts that any balanced presentation of the trivial group (n generators, n relations) can be reduced to the trivial presentation via a sequence of 'Andrews-Curtis moves': conjugations, inversions, and relator multiplications. Many researchers believe the conjecture is false, but no counterexample has been verified; several candidate counterexamples have been shown to actually be AC-trivializable with enough computation. The problem connects combinatorial group theory, topology (via handle decompositions), and the 4-dimensional smooth Poincaré conjecture.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Andrews-Curtis conjecture, widely suspected to be false. Our strategy combines (1) computational attack on proposed counterexamples using SAT solvers and reinforcement learning to search AC-move spaces, (2) development of algebraic obstructions distinguishing balanced presentations up to AC-equivalence, and (3) exploration of the topological link to 4D Poincaré. Either a definitive counterexample or a surprising confirmation would be a landmark in combinatorial group theory.

problems.title: Perfect Cuboid
problems.tagline: Does a rectangular box exist with all integer edges, face diagonals, and space diagonal?
problems.summary: A perfect cuboid has integer edges a,b,c, integer face diagonals, and integer space diagonal.
problems.description: A perfect cuboid is a rectangular parallelepiped with integer edge lengths a, b, c, integer face diagonals √(a²+b²), √(b²+c²), √(a²+c²), and integer space diagonal √(a²+b²+c²). Whether any such cuboid exists is a notorious open problem. Computer searches have confirmed that no perfect cuboid exists with smallest edge ≤ 10¹². The problem is equivalent to finding a rational point on a specific complete intersection of quadrics in ℙ⁶ — a variety with no obvious local obstructions. Partial cuboids (where all but one distance are integers) are well-known, but a perfect one has evaded discovery.
problems.abstract: We investigate the perfect cuboid problem — whether a rectangular box exists with all integer edges, face diagonals, and space diagonal. Our approach combines (1) exhaustive computational search using modular arithmetic sieving and elliptic-curve descent to push the verified bound beyond 10¹², (2) arithmetic geometry analysis of the variety defined by the system as an intersection of quadrics, seeking rational points or local-global obstructions, and (3) connection to Heegner-type constructions on related elliptic surfaces. Either existence or a structural non-existence proof would be celebrated.

problems.title: Hilbert's 16th Problem
problems.tagline: Bound the number of limit cycles of polynomial vector fields.
problems.summary: Find a uniform upper bound H(n) on the number of limit cycles of degree-n planar vector fields.
problems.description: The second part of Hilbert's 16th problem (1900) asks for an upper bound H(n) on the number of limit cycles of a polynomial vector field in the plane, as a function of the degree n. Even the existence of a finite bound was open until Ilyashenko and Écalle independently proved (in the early 1990s) that an individual polynomial vector field has only finitely many limit cycles (Dulac's problem). But a uniform bound H(n) remains completely open — even for n = 2 the best known bound is 4, and it is conjectured sharp. The problem has proven extraordinarily difficult.
problems.abstract: This project pursues Hilbert's 16th problem on limit cycles of polynomial planar vector fields. Our approach combines (1) refinement of the Ilyashenko-Écalle finiteness arguments with explicit effective bounds, (2) detailed bifurcation-theoretic analysis of n = 2 (quadratic) cases seeking confirmation of the conjectured H(2) = 4, and (3) tropical geometry and Newton polygon techniques for bounding cycle number via symbolic computation. Progress, even on quadratic case, would be a major event in dynamical systems.

problems.title: Invariant Subspace Problem
problems.tagline: Every bounded operator on a Banach space has a non-trivial invariant subspace.
problems.summary: Open for separable Banach spaces (disproved on ℓ¹ and c₀; open on Hilbert space).
problems.description: The invariant subspace problem asks: does every bounded linear operator T on an infinite-dimensional separable Banach space X have a non-trivial closed invariant subspace? Per Enflo (1975) and Charles Read (1984) independently constructed counterexamples on specific (non-Hilbert) Banach spaces — Enflo on ℓ¹, Read on c₀ and other spaces. The problem remains open on separable Hilbert space, and also on reflexive Banach spaces. It is arguably the most famous open problem in operator theory.
problems.abstract: This project addresses the invariant subspace problem on separable Hilbert space, the central remaining open case. Our approach combines (1) Lomonosov-type compact-operator arguments refined via Banach-algebra techniques, (2) analysis of specific problematic classes (hyponormal, hyper-cyclic) operators, and (3) C*-algebraic reformulations via universal invariant-subspace-lattice properties. While the general Hilbert-space case is extraordinarily difficult, new classes of operators satisfying the conjecture would constitute significant progress.

problems.title: Flint Hills Series Convergence
problems.tagline: Does Σ 1/(n³ sin²n) converge?
problems.summary: Convergence of the Flint Hills series Σ csc²(n)/n³ is open.
problems.description: The Flint Hills series is ∑_{n=1}^∞ csc²(n) / n³ = ∑ 1 / (n³ sin²(n)). Its convergence depends on the irrationality measure μ(π): convergent if μ(π) < 5/2, divergent if μ(π) ≥ 3. Current best bound gives μ(π) ≤ 7.6063 (Salikhov 2008) — far from the threshold. Thus we do not know whether the series converges. Related series like ∑ csc²(n)/n⁴ (Cookson Hills) have similar status. Resolving the convergence amounts to sharpening the irrationality measure of π — a problem in transcendental number theory that has resisted progress for decades.
problems.abstract: This project addresses the Flint Hills series Σ csc²(n)/n³, whose convergence hinges on the irrationality measure μ(π). Currently μ(π) ≤ 7.6063 (Salikhov), far from the critical threshold 5/2. Our approach combines (1) refinement of Padé-approximation methods in Salikhov's proof to push the bound below 7.6, (2) automorphic and modular-form input to continued-fraction-type expansions of π, and (3) detailed numerical study of partial sums to formulate sharper convergence heuristics. Progress advances transcendental number theory broadly.

problems.title: Ramsey Numbers R(5,5) and Beyond
problems.tagline: 43 ≤ R(5,5) ≤ 46 — determine the exact value.
problems.summary: The Ramsey number R(5,5) is trapped in a 4-value window; larger R(k,k) even harder.
problems.description: The Ramsey number R(k, ℓ) is the smallest n such that every red-blue edge-coloring of the complete graph K_n contains either a red K_k or a blue K_ℓ. Despite massive computational effort, R(5,5) is known only to lie in [43, 46]; the best lower bound was improved to 43 in 2017 by Exoo and Reynolds. Erdős famously remarked that if aliens demanded the value of R(5,5) to spare Earth, we should devote our entire civilization's computational resources to the problem — but if they asked for R(6,6), we should attempt to destroy them first. The asymptotic growth rate of R(k,k) is also unknown, with an exponential gap between known bounds.
problems.abstract: This project pursues both exact values and asymptotic bounds for Ramsey numbers. Our approach combines (1) refined SAT-solver and constraint-programming attacks on R(5,5), R(6,6), and small off-diagonal cases, (2) algebraic constructions (Paley graphs, cyclic constructions) to improve lower bounds, and (3) entropy-compression and probabilistic refinements of the Conlon upper bound for R(k,k). Intermediate targets include resolving R(5,5) within the known [43,46] window and sharpening asymptotic growth rates.

problems.title: Furstenberg ×2, ×3 Conjecture
problems.tagline: Closed sets invariant under both x→2x and x→3x mod 1 are finite or [0,1].
problems.summary: The only ×2,×3-invariant closed subsets of ℝ/ℤ are finite or the whole circle.
problems.description: Furstenberg's ×2, ×3 conjecture (1967) asserts that the only closed subsets of the unit circle ℝ/ℤ that are invariant under both multiplication-by-2 and multiplication-by-3 maps are either finite or the entire circle. Rudolph (1990) proved a measure-theoretic analog: any ×2,×3-invariant ergodic measure with positive entropy is Lebesgue — a celebrated result. The topological version of the conjecture (about closed sets) remains open. The problem is central to the theory of ×p,×q systems and arithmetic dynamics.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Furstenberg's ×2, ×3 conjecture on invariant closed sets in ℝ/ℤ. Our approach combines (1) extension of Rudolph-Host measure-theoretic methods to constrain topological invariants, (2) effective Diophantine approximation results to understand individual orbits under joint dynamics, and (3) exploitation of connections to the Littlewood conjecture and homogeneous dynamics of SL(3,ℝ)/SL(3,ℤ). A resolution would be a milestone in ergodic theory and arithmetic dynamics.

problems.title: Birkhoff's Billiards Conjecture
problems.tagline: Only ellipses produce integrable billiards.
problems.summary: A smooth strictly convex billiard table is integrable iff it is an ellipse.
problems.description: Birkhoff's billiards conjecture asserts that a smooth strictly convex planar billiard table whose dynamics are integrable (in the Liouville sense) must be an ellipse. The conjecture has been verified for perturbations of circles (Avila-De Simoi-Kaloshin 2016), for tables near ellipses, and under various integrability assumptions. The full conjecture remains open. The problem lies at the heart of integrable systems and rigidity in dynamical systems, with connections to spectral theory via the Kac problem ('can one hear the shape of a drum').
problems.abstract: This project pursues Birkhoff's billiards conjecture on integrability of convex billiards. Our strategy combines (1) perturbative KAM-style arguments extending Avila-De Simoi-Kaloshin to larger neighborhoods of the ellipse, (2) Aubry-Mather theory and extremal-orbit analysis, and (3) connection to spectral rigidity via the length spectrum. A successful proof would be a rigidity theorem at the heart of dynamical systems theory and illuminate the distinction between integrable and chaotic dynamics.

problems.title: Periodic Orbits in Triangular Billiards
problems.tagline: Does every obtuse triangle have a periodic billiard trajectory?
problems.summary: Periodic billiard orbits in acute triangles are known; obtuse case open.
problems.description: The question of whether every triangular billiard table admits a closed (periodic) orbit has been settled for acute and right triangles (Masur, and via Fagnano's orthic triangle for acute). For obtuse triangles, the problem remains open in general. Rich partial results exist: triangles with all angles rational multiples of π are covered by the theory of translation surfaces, and for some classes of irrational obtuse triangles specific orbits are known. The general obtuse case is a flagship open problem in billiard dynamics.
problems.abstract: This project targets periodic orbits in obtuse triangular billiards — the main remaining open case in the general question of existence of closed billiard paths in triangles. Our approach combines (1) translation-surface techniques for rational-angle triangles, (2) renormalization and continued-fraction methods for irrational angles, and (3) topological arguments via the Veech homomorphism and dichotomy. Progress would complete a classical question in billiards going back to Fagnano (1745).

problems.title: Quantum Unique Ergodicity
problems.tagline: High-energy eigenstates on negatively curved manifolds equidistribute.
problems.summary: On compact hyperbolic surfaces, L² mass of Laplacian eigenfunctions equidistributes in the limit.
problems.description: The Quantum Unique Ergodicity (QUE) conjecture, due to Rudnick and Sarnak, asserts that on a compact negatively curved Riemannian manifold, the probability measures associated with eigenfunctions of the Laplacian converge in the weak-* limit to the uniform (Liouville) measure as eigenvalues tend to infinity. Lindenstrauss proved arithmetic QUE for Hecke-Maass cusp forms on arithmetic hyperbolic surfaces (2006 Fields Medal). The general (non-arithmetic) case remains open. QUE has deep connections to L-functions, automorphic forms, and subconvexity.
problems.abstract: This project pursues QUE (Quantum Unique Ergodicity) beyond the arithmetic case settled by Lindenstrauss. Our approach combines (1) extension of measure rigidity methods from homogeneous dynamics to more general negatively curved manifolds, (2) entropy-bound techniques for semiclassical measures, and (3) automorphic input for hyperbolic surfaces with rich symmetries. Full non-arithmetic QUE would be a landmark in mathematical physics, connecting eigenfunction analysis to ergodic and spectral theory.

problems.title: Generalized Moonshine Conjecture
problems.tagline: Monstrous moonshine extends to all pairs (g, h) of commuting Monster elements.
problems.summary: Each commuting pair (g,h) ⊂ M determines a modular function (generalized McKay-Thompson series).
problems.description: Generalized moonshine, formulated by Norton following Borcherds' proof of Conway-Norton monstrous moonshine (1998 Fields Medal), asserts that for each pair of commuting elements (g, h) in the Monster group M, there is a holomorphic function f(g,h)(τ) satisfying properties generalizing Monstrous Moonshine. Much of the structure was made precise via orbifold vertex operator algebras; Carnahan (2012) proved the conjecture assuming certain technical results. The full, unconditional proof with all details verified remains a frontier question.
problems.abstract: This project investigates generalized moonshine beyond Borcherds' foundational proof of monstrous moonshine. Our approach combines (1) completion of Carnahan's orbifold-theoretic framework with full rigor on all technical conditions, (2) connections to topological modular forms and umbral moonshine phenomena, and (3) Monster group representation theory augmented by recent progress in vertex operator algebras. The work connects finite group theory, modular forms, and mathematical physics in deep and surprising ways.

problems.title: abc Conjecture
problems.tagline: c cannot greatly exceed rad(abc) for a + b = c coprime.
problems.summary: For coprime a+b=c, c ≤ K(ε) · rad(abc)^{1+ε} for all ε > 0.
problems.description: The abc conjecture (Masser-Oesterlé, 1985) states that for any ε > 0, there exists a constant K(ε) such that for all coprime positive integers a, b, c with a + b = c, we have c ≤ K(ε) · rad(abc)^{1+ε}, where rad(n) is the product of distinct primes dividing n. The conjecture has stunning consequences throughout number theory: Fermat's Last Theorem for large exponents, Mordell's conjecture, Szpiro's conjecture, Roth's theorem improvements, and many Diophantine statements. Shinichi Mochizuki announced a proof in 2012 using 'Inter-universal Teichmüller Theory', but the mathematical community remains deeply divided on whether the proof is correct; the dispute is ongoing as of 2025.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the abc conjecture, whose proof status remains disputed after Mochizuki's 2012 announcement. Our approach does not attempt to verify or refute IUT, but instead pursues alternative approaches: (1) modular and automorphic input via Szpiro-type inequalities, (2) height-theoretic refinements via Arakelov intersection theory, and (3) specific quantitative improvements on effective Mordell conjecture bounds that approach abc in strength. Progress provides both evidence and alternative routes toward this central conjecture.

problems.title: Brocard's Problem
problems.tagline: Only three solutions to n! + 1 = m² are known.
problems.summary: n! + 1 = m² has only solutions n = 4, 5, 7 (known); no more exist.
problems.description: Brocard's problem (1876) asks for integer solutions to the equation n! + 1 = m². Only three are known: (n, m) = (4, 5), (5, 11), (7, 71). Brocard conjectured these are the only solutions; this is sometimes called Brocard's conjecture. Computational verification reaches n = 10⁹. The problem is notoriously resistant: elementary Diophantine methods give essentially no traction, and no connection to known structural tools (like abc or elliptic curves) has yielded unconditional progress. Under abc conjecture, only finitely many solutions exist.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Brocard's problem n! + 1 = m², conjectured to have only three solutions. Our approach combines (1) computational verification extension beyond n = 10⁹ using modular arithmetic optimizations and distributed computing, (2) connection to abc conjecture consequences for Diophantine factorial equations, and (3) analytic methods based on the distribution of square values of n! + 1. While a full proof likely awaits new structural insight, intermediate progress extends the known negative range and clarifies the problem's relation to other conjectures.

problems.title: Kummer-Vandiver Conjecture
problems.tagline: For every odd prime p, p does not divide h⁺(ℚ(ζ_p)).
problems.summary: The class number of the maximal real subfield of ℚ(ζ_p) is prime to p.
problems.description: The Kummer-Vandiver conjecture asserts that for every odd prime p, the class number h⁺ of the maximal totally real subfield of the cyclotomic field ℚ(ζ_p) is not divisible by p. Equivalently, certain Iwasawa-theoretic invariants vanish. The conjecture has been verified computationally for all primes p < 163 × 10⁶, and it is consistent with all predictions of the Main Conjecture of Iwasawa theory. However, no theoretical proof exists, and deep theoretical reasons for its truth are unclear. Washington and others have speculated that it might even be false for rare primes, but none are known.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Kummer-Vandiver conjecture — verified for an enormous range but lacking any theoretical proof. Our approach combines (1) extension of computational verification using modern techniques in Iwasawa theory, (2) analytic estimates for p-divisibility of h⁺ via Bernoulli number congruences and p-adic L-function values, and (3) structural study of eigenspace decompositions under Galois action. Progress clarifies the theoretical basis for an empirically verified but mysterious conjecture central to classical and Iwasawa number theory.

problems.title: Infinitude of Wieferich Primes
problems.tagline: Only two Wieferich primes known: 1093 and 3511.
problems.summary: Are there infinitely many primes p with 2^{p-1} ≡ 1 (mod p²)?
problems.description: A Wieferich prime is an odd prime p such that 2^{p-1} ≡ 1 (mod p²) (rather than just mod p, which is Fermat's little theorem). Only two are known: 1093 (Meissner 1913) and 3511 (Beeger 1922). Exhaustive computation extends beyond 6.7 × 10¹⁷ with no further examples. Heuristically, the density of Wieferich primes up to x should be ~ log log x, so infinitely many are expected but exceedingly sparse. The conjecture that infinitely many exist remains open, as does even the conjecture that infinitely many non-Wieferich primes exist (implied by the abc conjecture).
problems.abstract: This project investigates Wieferich primes — primes p with 2^{p-1} ≡ 1 (mod p²). Only two are known despite exhaustive search beyond 6.7 × 10¹⁷. Our approach combines (1) distributed computational search using optimized modular exponentiation on GPU clusters, (2) analytic density heuristics via random models and their consequences, and (3) connection to abc conjecture which implies infinitude of non-Wieferich primes. Discovery of a third example or new theoretical constraints would be a landmark.

problems.title: Marshall Hall's Conjecture
problems.tagline: |x³ - y²| is rarely very small.
problems.summary: For nonzero integer solutions, |x³ - y²| ≫ x^{1/2 - ε}.
problems.description: Marshall Hall's conjecture (1971) asserts that for any ε > 0, there are only finitely many integer pairs (x, y) with x³ ≠ y² and |x³ - y²| < x^{1/2 - ε}. This is a Diophantine statement about how closely a perfect cube can approximate a perfect square. It is implied by the abc conjecture and is essentially equivalent to effective bounds on integral points on Mordell curves y² = x³ + k. Best unconditional results are far from the conjectured threshold. Known 'good' examples with small |x³ - y²| are rare and have been extensively cataloged.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Marshall Hall's conjecture on x³ - y² differences. Our approach combines (1) computer search for extremal examples (cases with small |x³ - y²|), (2) effective bounds for integral points on Mordell curves via linear forms in logarithms, and (3) connection to abc conjecture and other Diophantine statements. While a full resolution likely depends on abc or equivalent tools, sharpening effective bounds has immediate number-theoretic applications.

problems.title: Pillai's Conjecture
problems.tagline: The equation |a^x - b^y| = c has finitely many solutions.
problems.summary: For fixed a, b, c with a, b ≥ 2, |a^x - b^y| = c has finitely many (x, y) in positive integers.
problems.description: Pillai's conjecture (1931) states that for any fixed positive integers a, b ≥ 2 and c ≥ 1, the equation |a^x - b^y| = c has only finitely many solutions in positive integers (x, y). For c = 1 this is the Catalan conjecture, proved by Mihailescu in 2002 (the only solution is 3² - 2³ = 1). The general case remains open. Pillai's conjecture is implied by the abc conjecture. Special cases are known (e.g., when a, b are primes), but the general statement has resisted unconditional proof.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Pillai's conjecture generalizing Catalan's theorem. Our approach combines (1) Baker-type linear forms in logarithms to bound solutions explicitly for specific (a, b, c), (2) arithmetic geometry of S-unit equations, and (3) analytic connections to abc via Szpiro-type arguments. Intermediate results include new effective bounds for specific families and understanding of the distribution of exceptional solutions. Full resolution likely requires abc-strength tools.

problems.title: Dixmier Conjecture
problems.tagline: Every endomorphism of the Weyl algebra is an automorphism.
problems.summary: An endomorphism of the first Weyl algebra A₁(k) is automatically an automorphism.
problems.description: The Dixmier conjecture (1968) asserts that every endomorphism of the first Weyl algebra A₁(k) over a field of characteristic zero is automatically an automorphism. This is equivalent to the Jacobian conjecture in two variables (and in fact is equivalent to the Jacobian conjecture in all dimensions via stable equivalence). The conjecture therefore shares the notorious difficulty of the Jacobian conjecture and attracts many 'proofs' that are later shown to be incorrect. The problem lies at the heart of noncommutative algebra.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Dixmier conjecture — equivalent to the Jacobian conjecture via stable equivalence results. Our approach combines (1) noncommutative methods using quantum calculus and the structure of A₁(k)-modules, (2) degree-bound analysis for polynomial endomorphisms via the Mathieu subspace, and (3) computer verification of specific low-degree cases. As with the Jacobian conjecture, progress requires care to avoid the many pitfalls that have invalidated prior attempts.

problems.title: Serre's Conjecture II
