problems.tagline: Semisimple simply connected groups have trivial Galois cohomology over cohomological dimension ≤ 2 fields.
problems.summary: H¹(k, G) = 0 for G semisimple simply connected, k of cohomological dim ≤ 2.
problems.description: Serre's Conjecture II asserts that for a field k of cohomological dimension ≤ 2 (such as a totally imaginary number field) and a semisimple simply connected linear algebraic group G over k, the Galois cohomology H¹(k, G) is trivial. The conjecture has been verified for many types of groups (via work of Bayer-Parimala, Chernousov, Gille, and others) but remains open for groups of type E₈ in general and some other specific cases. It is a central problem in Galois cohomology and algebraic groups.
problems.abstract: This project addresses Serre's Conjecture II for remaining difficult types, particularly E₈. Our approach combines (1) techniques from the theory of algebraic groups, especially the structure of quasi-split forms over local and global fields, (2) norm residue symbol methods in higher-dimensional class field theory, and (3) Gille-Parimala hybrid arguments adapted to exceptional types. Resolution for E₈ would essentially complete the conjecture.

problems.title: Abundance Conjecture
problems.tagline: On a minimal model, the canonical divisor is semiample if it is nef.
problems.summary: For a minimal variety X, K_X nef implies K_X semiample.
problems.description: The abundance conjecture asserts that on a minimal model X (smooth projective variety with K_X nef), the canonical divisor K_X is semiample — i.e., some multiple |mK_X| defines a morphism. This is a central unsolved problem in the Minimal Model Program (MMP) of Mori, Reid, Kawamata, Kollár, and others. It is known in dimensions ≤ 3 and for many specific classes. In higher dimensions it remains open and is intimately connected with log-canonical threshold theory, boundedness of singularities, and the existence of good models.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the abundance conjecture for minimal models in dimension ≥ 4 — the central remaining piece of the Minimal Model Program. Our approach combines (1) extension of the non-vanishing theorem via multiplier ideal sheaves, (2) boundedness results for log-canonical thresholds, and (3) the 4-dimensional case via specific singular models. Progress completes a research program initiated by Mori in the 1980s and has sweeping consequences for the structure of algebraic varieties.

problems.title: Bass's Conjecture
problems.tagline: K₀(ℤ[G]) is finitely generated for finitely generated G.
problems.summary: For every finitely generated group G, the algebraic K₀ of its integral group ring is f.g.
problems.description: Bass's conjecture (in one form) asserts that for any finitely generated group G, the K-theory K₀(ℤ[G]) is a finitely generated abelian group. A closely related statement concerns traces and the structure of K₀. The conjecture has deep connections to topology (via Farrell-Jones) and is known for many classes (virtually nilpotent groups, CAT(0) groups) but open in general. It is a central problem linking algebraic topology and algebraic K-theory.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Bass's conjecture on K₀ of integral group rings. Our approach combines (1) extension of Farrell-Jones conjecture techniques to more general group classes, (2) controlled topology and assembly-map methods, and (3) geometric group theory inputs for new classes of groups. Progress connects algebraic K-theory, geometric topology, and combinatorial group theory.

problems.title: Arnold-Givental Conjecture
problems.tagline: Lagrangian Floer homology recovers ordinary homology for monotone Lagrangians.
problems.summary: For certain Lagrangian submanifolds L, #(L ∩ φ(L)) ≥ sum of Betti numbers of L (mod 2).
problems.description: The Arnold-Givental conjecture asserts that for a compact Lagrangian submanifold L of a symplectic manifold, fixed by an antisymplectic involution, the number of intersections of L with its image under a Hamiltonian isotopy is bounded below by the sum of ℤ/2-Betti numbers of L. This is a Lagrangian version of the Arnold conjecture on symplectic fixed points. It is known in many cases via Floer homology, but the general statement remains open. Resolution requires further development of Lagrangian Floer theory.
problems.abstract: This project pursues the Arnold-Givental conjecture via development of Lagrangian Floer homology. Our strategy combines (1) virtual fundamental class techniques for Floer homology on exotic Lagrangians, (2) Fukaya-category methods to extract intersection lower bounds, and (3) case-by-case analysis for specific symplectic manifolds. Progress advances symplectic topology and the foundations of mirror symmetry.

problems.title: Kervaire Invariant Problem
problems.tagline: In which dimensions does the Kervaire invariant detect framed bordism?
problems.summary: Kervaire invariant one exists only in dimensions 2, 6, 14, 30, 62 and possibly 126.
problems.description: The Kervaire invariant problem asks in which dimensions n there exists a framed n-manifold of Kervaire invariant one. Hill, Hopkins, and Ravenel (2016) proved that such manifolds exist only in dimensions 2, 6, 14, 30, 62, and possibly 126 — a historic breakthrough that settled a problem open since the 1960s. The case n = 126 remains open: it is the single remaining dimension where existence is unresolved. The problem sits at the heart of stable homotopy theory and differential topology.
problems.abstract: This project addresses the remaining Kervaire invariant case in dimension 126 following the Hill-Hopkins-Ravenel theorem. Our approach combines (1) refined computation of the Adams spectral sequence at the relevant stem, (2) EHP-sequence analysis to distinguish the n = 126 case from the ruled-out higher dimensions, and (3) equivariant homotopy theory techniques extending HHR. The remaining case involves delicate computation that may require new theoretical tools.

problems.title: Landau and Bloch Constants
problems.tagline: Exact values of Landau's constant L and Bloch's constant B are unknown.
problems.summary: Best known bounds for L and B fall short of conjectured values.
problems.description: Landau's and Bloch's constants concern the size of disks in the image of holomorphic functions from the unit disk. The Bloch constant B is the largest number such that every holomorphic function on the unit disk with f'(0) = 1 has a univalent subregion mapped onto a disk of radius B. Bloch (1925) proved B ≥ √3/4; the conjectured exact value is (√3/4) · Γ(1/3)·Γ(11/12) / Γ(1/4) · √(√3 - 1) ≈ 0.4719, due to Ahlfors-Grunsky. Best known upper bound is marginally above. Landau's constant L has similar status. Exact values require entirely new methods.
problems.abstract: This project targets exact values of the Landau and Bloch constants. Current bounds fall just short of the Ahlfors-Grunsky conjectured values. Our approach combines (1) analysis of extremal Riemann surfaces via refined variational methods, (2) Beltrami-equation techniques to study candidate extremal maps, and (3) computer-assisted proofs via rigorous interval arithmetic. Even marginal improvements would represent progress on problems open since the 1920s.

problems.title: Fuglede's Spectral Set Conjecture
problems.tagline: Spectral sets are exactly the tiles of ℝⁿ.
problems.summary: A bounded measurable Ω ⊂ ℝⁿ is spectral iff Ω tiles ℝⁿ by translations.
problems.description: Fuglede's conjecture (1974) asserts that a bounded measurable subset Ω ⊂ ℝⁿ of positive measure admits an orthogonal basis of exponentials e^{2πi⟨λ,x⟩} (i.e., is a spectral set) if and only if Ω tiles ℝⁿ by translations. Tao (2004) disproved both directions in dimensions ≥ 5, and the failure was pushed down through the work of various authors to dimensions ≥ 3. In dimensions 1 and 2 the conjecture is still open in full generality, and specific cases (convex bodies, low-dimensional cases) are being studied. The problem connects harmonic analysis, combinatorics, and geometry.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Fuglede's spectral set conjecture in dimensions 1 and 2 — the remaining open cases. Our approach combines (1) combinatorial analysis of tiling sets in ℤ, leveraging recent progress via generating polynomial methods, (2) convex-body specific analysis using Minkowski and Brunn theorems, and (3) computer search for potential counterexamples. Progress clarifies the interplay between tiling, spectrality, and number-theoretic properties of the set of tiling lengths.

problems.title: Van der Waerden Numbers
problems.tagline: Compute W(k, r): smallest N with every r-coloring of [N] containing a monochromatic length-k AP.
problems.summary: Only W(k, r) for tiny (k, r) are known; growth rate poorly understood.
problems.description: The van der Waerden number W(k, r) is the smallest integer N such that every r-coloring of {1, 2, ..., N} contains a monochromatic arithmetic progression of length k. Van der Waerden's theorem (1927) guarantees W(k, r) is finite. Exact values are known only for very small cases (e.g., W(3, 2) = 9, W(4, 2) = 35, W(5, 2) = 178; W(3, 3) = 27; W(3, 4) = 76; W(4, 3) = 293; a few more). Growth rates: Gowers' proof of Szemerédi's theorem gives W(k, r) ≤ 2^{2^{r^{2^{k^{2^{k+9}}}}}}, later improved. True growth is believed to be much smaller but not established. The problem is a flagship challenge in combinatorial Ramsey theory.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Van der Waerden numbers W(k, r). Our approach combines (1) massive parallel SAT-solver computation to extend known values of W(k, r) to more cases, (2) explicit constructions to improve lower bounds via affine-geometry techniques, and (3) improved upper bounds via the density Hales-Jewett theorem and its quantitative versions. Even marginal numerical progress has high combinatorial interest given the extreme computational difficulty.

problems.title: Self-Avoiding Walk Exact Counting
problems.tagline: Find the exact generating function of self-avoiding walks on ℤ².
problems.summary: The connective constant μ of self-avoiding walks on ℤ² is unknown in closed form.
problems.description: A self-avoiding walk (SAW) on ℤ² is a lattice path that visits no vertex twice. The number c_n of SAWs of length n grows as c_n ≈ A · μ^n · n^{γ-1}, where μ is the connective constant (specific to lattice), γ is a critical exponent (conjecturally γ = 43/32 for ℤ²), and A is a lattice-dependent constant. On the hexagonal lattice, Smirnov (2010) proved the conjectured value μ = √(2 + √2). On ℤ², the exact value is unknown; numerical estimate is μ ≈ 2.63815853. The SAW model is central to statistical mechanics and conformal field theory.
problems.abstract: This project investigates exact results for self-avoiding walks on ℤ². Following Smirnov's proof on the hexagonal lattice, our approach combines (1) conformal field theory predictions via SLE_{8/3} analysis, (2) parafermionic observable methods extending Smirnov-Duminil-Copin to square lattices, and (3) high-precision Monte Carlo simulation to constrain candidate exact values. Full determination of the connective constant on ℤ² would be a landmark in statistical mechanics.

problems.title: Rokhlin's Multiple Mixing Problem
problems.tagline: Does strong mixing imply higher-order mixing?
problems.summary: For measure-preserving systems, does 2-mixing imply k-mixing for all k?
problems.description: Rokhlin's multiple mixing problem (1949) asks whether a measure-preserving transformation T that is mixing (i.e., strongly mixing of order 2) is automatically mixing of all orders. Host (1991) proved this is true for pairwise independent transformations with certain spectral properties, and for many natural classes. Kalikow, Ryzhikov, and others have contributed partial results. The general problem remains open despite 75 years of work. It touches the structure theory of dynamical systems and is central to modern ergodic theory.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Rokhlin's multiple mixing problem. Our approach combines (1) joining-theoretic analysis following del Junco-Rudolph and later work by Host, (2) spectral theory for ergodic flows with carefully designed multiplicity functions, and (3) construction of potential counterexamples via explicit rank-one transformations. Progress has consequences for the spectral theory of dynamical systems and the classification of measure-preserving transformations.

problems.title: Margulis's Conjecture on Lattices
problems.tagline: Classify stationary measures on homogeneous spaces under arithmetic groups.
problems.summary: Classification of invariant measures for actions of higher-rank arithmetic groups.
problems.description: Margulis's conjectures concerning the dynamics of arithmetic lattices on homogeneous spaces have been a central driving force in ergodic theory for decades. Ratner's theorems (1991) resolved the measure-classification conjectures for unipotent flows. Further Margulis-style questions concerning measure rigidity for higher-rank actions, stationary measures, and actions of arithmetic groups on moduli spaces remain active frontiers. Eskin-Mirzakhani's work on SL(2,ℝ) actions on moduli space of quadratic differentials represents the latest major progress.
problems.abstract: This project pursues Margulis-type measure rigidity results for higher-rank actions on homogeneous and moduli spaces. Our approach combines (1) extension of Eskin-Mirzakhani techniques to broader classes of group actions, (2) entropy-rigidity theorems for stationary measures, and (3) connections to Diophantine approximation via Oppenheim-type conjectures. Progress lies at the intersection of ergodic theory, number theory, and Lie theory.

problems.title: Hilbert-Arnold Problem
problems.tagline: Effective bound on limit cycles for polynomial families.
problems.summary: A uniform bound on limit cycles for polynomial vector fields of fixed degree.
problems.description: The Hilbert-Arnold problem is the finiteness version of Hilbert's 16th problem: given a family of polynomial vector fields, is there a uniform bound on the number of limit cycles, depending only on the degree and the family? While individual polynomial vector fields have finitely many limit cycles (Dulac), a uniform bound across families remains open. The problem is 126 years old. It connects to ASRP research through differential equations modeling physical systems.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Hilbert-Arnold problem on uniform bounds for limit cycles in families of polynomial vector fields. Our approach combines (1) effective versions of Dulac's finiteness theorem with quantitative estimates depending only on degree, (2) bifurcation analysis of limit-cycle cascades in specific families, and (3) analytic continuation methods for abelian integrals. The work has direct relevance to ASRP research on physical differential-equation models where effective cycle bounds are needed.

problems.title: Catalan-Dickson Conjecture (Aliquot Sequences)
problems.tagline: All aliquot sequences terminate.
problems.summary: The aliquot sequence starting from every n is eventually periodic or reaches 0.
problems.description: The Catalan-Dickson conjecture asserts that every aliquot sequence (iteration of the sum-of-proper-divisors function s(n) = σ(n) - n) is eventually periodic — either reaching 0, a perfect number (fixed point), an amicable pair (2-cycle), or a longer cycle. Several starting values have sequences that have been computed for millions of iterations without reaching any known termination (the 'Lehmer five': 276, 552, 564, 660, 966). Whether these eventually terminate or grow forever is unknown. Even the theoretical foundations of amicable numbers and aliquot cycles are poorly developed.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Catalan-Dickson conjecture on aliquot sequences. Our approach combines (1) massive-parallel computation extending the 'Lehmer five' open sequences beyond current bounds, (2) probabilistic heuristics based on σ(n)/n distribution to predict long-term behavior, and (3) structural analysis of amicable-type cycles and their density. Progress either finds terminating points for Lehmer sequences or establishes rigorous divergence bounds.

problems.title: Grimm's Conjecture
problems.tagline: Consecutive composites share distinct prime divisors.
problems.summary: If n+1, ..., n+k are all composite, they admit distinct prime divisors p_i | n+i.
problems.description: Grimm's conjecture (1969) asserts that if n + 1, n + 2, ..., n + k are all composite, then there exist distinct prime numbers p_1, ..., p_k such that p_i divides n + i for each i. The conjecture is elementary but exceptionally hard — no nontrivial upper bound on the length k of such a system is known under current techniques. It is a kind of matching condition between prime factorization and consecutive integers. Best known unconditional results give very weak bounds.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Grimm's conjecture on distinct prime divisors of consecutive composites. Our approach combines (1) Erdős-style elementary methods refined with modern sieve theory, (2) connection to smooth number distribution and the Turán-Kubilius inequality, and (3) specific computational exploration of extremal cases. Progress has consequences for the distribution of smooth numbers and the structure of consecutive-integer factorizations.

problems.title: Casas-Alvero Conjecture
problems.tagline: Polynomials sharing roots with all derivatives are powers of linear forms.
problems.summary: If f ∈ k[x] shares a root with each derivative f^(i) (0 ≤ i < deg f), then f = c(x-a)^n.
problems.description: The Casas-Alvero conjecture (2001) asserts that a polynomial f of degree n over a field of characteristic 0 that shares a root with each of its derivatives f, f', f'', ..., f^{(n-1)} (not necessarily the same root) must be a constant times a pure power (x - a)^n. In characteristic p > 0 the conjecture is false. The conjecture has been verified in low degrees and in many specific cases, but the general case in characteristic 0 remains open. It is a surprisingly deep question linking polynomial structure to root-derivative relations.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Casas-Alvero conjecture in characteristic 0. Our approach combines (1) degree-by-degree verification via Gröbner basis computation and elimination theory, (2) differential-algebraic analysis of the polynomial-derivative resultant, and (3) connections to ramification in p-adic settings to distinguish characteristic-0 from characteristic-p behavior. Progress requires new tools beyond elementary polynomial methods.

problems.title: Kaplansky's Conjectures on Group Rings
problems.tagline: Torsion-free group rings have no zero divisors, idempotents, or units.
problems.summary: For torsion-free group G and field K, K[G] has no nontrivial zero divisors, idempotents, or units.
problems.description: Kaplansky's conjectures (1940s) on the group ring K[G] of a torsion-free group G over a field K consist of three interrelated statements: (1) zero divisor conjecture — K[G] has no nontrivial zero divisors; (2) idempotent conjecture — K[G] has no nontrivial idempotents; (3) unit conjecture — the only units of K[G] are trivial (scalar times group element). The unit conjecture was FALSIFIED in 2021 by Giles Gardam for the Hantzsche-Wendt group in characteristic 2. The zero divisor and idempotent conjectures remain open for general torsion-free groups. These problems connect group theory, ring theory, and topology (via L²-invariants).
problems.abstract: This project investigates Kaplansky's remaining conjectures on group rings following Gardam's 2021 disproof of the unit conjecture. Our approach combines (1) L²-homology methods via Lück-Linnell techniques for the zero divisor conjecture, (2) analysis of specific candidate counterexample groups beyond the Hantzsche-Wendt case, and (3) computer search in characteristic-0 settings. Progress clarifies the boundary between statements that hold and those that fail for torsion-free groups.

problems.title: Bass-Quillen Conjecture
problems.tagline: Projective modules over R[x₁,...,xₙ] extend from R if R is regular.
problems.summary: For R regular local, every f.g. projective R[t₁,...,tₙ]-module is extended from R.
problems.description: The Bass-Quillen conjecture asserts that if R is a regular ring and P is a finitely generated projective module over the polynomial ring R[t₁, ..., tₙ], then P is extended from R — i.e., P ≅ (P/tP) ⊗_R R[t₁, ..., tₙ]. Quillen and Suslin independently proved the case R = field (the 'Serre problem'). Lindel proved the conjecture for R a regular local ring of geometric type. The general case remains open. Resolution would complete a major chapter in commutative algebra and algebraic K-theory.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Bass-Quillen conjecture for general regular local rings. Our approach combines (1) extension of the Quillen-Suslin-Lindel techniques to mixed-characteristic regular local rings, (2) application of prismatic cohomology and new derived-algebraic-geometry tools, and (3) consideration of specific singular but mild cases to identify the exact scope of the conjecture. Resolution lies at the intersection of commutative algebra, algebraic geometry, and K-theory.

problems.title: Fujita's Conjecture
problems.tagline: K_X + (n+1)L is base-point-free on smooth projective varieties.
problems.summary: For X smooth projective of dim n and L ample, K_X + (n+1)L is base-point-free; K_X + (n+2)L is very ample.
problems.description: Fujita's conjecture (1985) asserts that for a smooth projective variety X of dimension n and an ample line bundle L on X, the adjoint bundle K_X + (n+1)L is base-point-free, and K_X + (n+2)L is very ample. Resolution would provide effective bounds in algebraic geometry. Known in dimensions n ≤ 5 through the work of Reider (n = 2), Ein-Lazarsfeld, Kawamata, Fujino, and others. For n ≥ 6 the conjecture remains open. It is central to effective birational geometry.
problems.abstract: This project pursues Fujita's conjecture in dimension n ≥ 6. Our approach combines (1) refinement of multiplier ideal sheaf techniques following Angehrn-Siu, (2) new vanishing theorems for adjoint bundles, and (3) geometric analysis of the base locus via Nakayama's method. Progress would establish effective bounds on adjunction, with wide implications throughout birational geometry.

problems.title: Green's Conjecture on Syzygies
problems.tagline: Syzygies of canonical curves are determined by Clifford index.
problems.summary: The Koszul cohomology of a canonically embedded curve vanishes as predicted by Clifford index.
problems.description: Green's conjecture on syzygies of canonical curves asserts that the Koszul cohomology groups K_{p,q}(X, K_X) vanish as dictated by the Clifford index of the curve X. Voisin (2002, 2005) proved the generic case for each genus. Recent work by Aprodu, Farkas, and others has extended the conjecture to additional cases. The conjecture remains open in full generality (for all curves, not just generic). It is central to the study of algebraic curves and their embeddings.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Green's syzygy conjecture for non-generic curves. Our approach combines (1) extension of Voisin's Koszul cohomology techniques to specific non-generic classes (e.g., curves on K3 surfaces), (2) Hilbert scheme and Brill-Noether methods, and (3) computer-assisted verification on specific curves via Macaulay2. Progress advances the classical theory of syzygies and has wide-ranging consequences in the geometry of canonical curves.

problems.title: Zariski-Lipman Conjecture
problems.tagline: Free tangent sheaf implies smoothness.
problems.summary: A variety with locally free tangent sheaf is smooth.
problems.description: The Zariski-Lipman conjecture asserts that a complex algebraic variety X (or more generally a Noetherian local ring) whose tangent sheaf is locally free (i.e., free on the smooth locus and extending to all of X) must be smooth. Known in low dimensions and for many specific classes (hypersurfaces, complete intersections). Open in general. It is a rigidity statement about the tangent bundle controlling smoothness.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Zariski-Lipman conjecture. Our approach combines (1) resolution of singularities methods applied to the tangent bundle extension, (2) derivations and Lie-theoretic structure on the module of differentials, and (3) specific classes of non-toric mildly singular varieties as test cases. Progress clarifies how closely tangent sheaf structure can detect singularities.

problems.title: Euler Equations Regularity
problems.tagline: Do smooth 3D Euler solutions form singularities in finite time?
problems.summary: Global existence/regularity for 3D incompressible Euler from smooth initial data.
problems.description: The 3D incompressible Euler equations govern inviscid fluid flow. Whether smooth initial data always yields global smooth solutions, or whether finite-time singularity formation ('blow-up') can occur, is a central open problem in PDE theory, on par with the Navier-Stokes regularity (Millennium) problem. Beale-Kato-Majda criterion provides a necessary condition for blow-up. Recent numerical evidence by Hou and others has suggested candidate blow-up scenarios with axial symmetry, culminating in the 2022 announcement by Chen-Hou of a computer-assisted proof of self-similar blow-up for 3D Euler with boundary, though full rigorous validation is ongoing.
problems.abstract: This project investigates finite-time singularity formation in 3D Euler equations. Building on Chen-Hou's computer-assisted proof for the boundary case, our approach combines (1) completion and verification of self-similar profile analysis, (2) extension to the boundary-free case via careful analytical arguments, and (3) alternative approaches via vorticity alignment and structure function methods. Resolution of Euler regularity is a prerequisite for Navier-Stokes and lies at the heart of fluid dynamics.

problems.title: Vlasov-Maxwell Regularity
problems.tagline: Global regularity for the relativistic Vlasov-Maxwell system.
problems.summary: Does Vlasov-Maxwell admit global smooth solutions from smooth initial data in 3D?
problems.description: The Vlasov-Maxwell system describes the evolution of charged particle distributions coupled with self-consistent electromagnetic fields. Whether the 3D system admits global smooth solutions for arbitrary smooth initial data is an important unresolved PDE problem. Glassey-Strauss (1986) proved conditional regularity under compact momentum support assumptions. Progress by Luk-Strain and others has extended regularity under various conditions, but the unconditional case remains open.
problems.abstract: This project pursues global regularity for the 3D relativistic Vlasov-Maxwell system. Our approach combines (1) refinement of null-condition analysis for Maxwell fields, (2) moment estimates on particle distribution, and (3) averaging methods for the Vlasov equation with improved dispersive bounds. Progress connects kinetic theory, PDE analysis, and mathematical physics.

problems.title: Perfect Graph Problems (Refinements)
problems.tagline: Recognition and structure beyond the Strong Perfect Graph Theorem.
problems.summary: Polynomial recognition, χ-binding functions, and refinements remain open.
problems.description: The Strong Perfect Graph Theorem (Chudnovsky-Robertson-Seymour-Thomas, 2006) resolved the characterization of perfect graphs. Further refinements and structural questions remain: efficient recognition algorithms, χ-binding functions (how much larger can χ be than ω for specific classes), and detailed structure of 'almost perfect' graphs. While the foundational theorem is proved, these follow-up problems pose significant challenges and are active research.
problems.abstract: This project investigates refinements and extensions of the Strong Perfect Graph Theorem. Our approach combines (1) efficient algorithmic recognition of perfect graphs with improved complexity bounds, (2) χ-boundedness questions for hereditary graph classes, and (3) structural theorems for almost-perfect graphs. Progress has algorithmic consequences and refines the landscape of graph coloring problems.

problems.title: Ringel's Tree Packing Conjecture
problems.tagline: K_{2n+1} decomposes into 2n+1 copies of any tree T with n edges.
problems.summary: Every tree with n edges packs 2n+1 times into K_{2n+1}.
problems.description: Ringel's conjecture (1963) asserts that for every tree T with n edges, the complete graph K_{2n+1} can be decomposed into 2n+1 edge-disjoint copies of T. Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov (2020) proved the conjecture for all sufficiently large n using ingenious probabilistic methods. The remaining cases involve smaller n and are more delicate, though in principle computationally accessible. A fully unconditional proof for all n is pending.
problems.abstract: This project pursues Ringel's tree packing conjecture for small n — the remaining gap after the Montgomery-Pokrovskiy-Sudakov asymptotic result. Our approach combines (1) explicit construction methods for specific families of small trees, (2) computer-assisted verification via SAT encoding, and (3) refinement of the MPS probabilistic argument to yield explicit small bounds. Closing the remaining gap completes a 60-year-old conjecture.

problems.title: Erdős-Faber-Lovász Conjecture
problems.tagline: n near-disjoint cliques on n vertices each are n-edge-colorable.
problems.summary: The union of n edge-disjoint complete graphs on n vertices has chromatic index ≤ n.
problems.description: The Erdős-Faber-Lovász conjecture (1972) asserts that if G is the union of n edge-disjoint complete graphs K_n, each with n vertices, where any two of the complete graphs share at most one vertex, then the chromatic number χ(G) ≤ n. Kang, Kelly, Kühn, Methuku, and Osthus (2023) proved the conjecture for all sufficiently large n. The remaining cases are for small n and are expected to follow from existing techniques, though a complete unconditional proof is still pending.
problems.abstract: This project pursues the Erdős-Faber-Lovász conjecture for small n, following the Kang-Kelly-Kühn-Methuku-Osthus asymptotic result. Our approach combines (1) case-by-case verification for small n via SAT-solver encoding of the chromatic-number constraint, (2) refinement of the KKKMO absorbing-path method to yield explicit small bounds, and (3) exploration of extremal configurations. Full resolution completes a 50-year-old conjecture in graph coloring.

problems.title: Set-Theoretic Union Chain Condition
problems.tagline: Chain conditions on unions in forcing and large cardinal theory.
problems.summary: Structural questions on unions of chains under forcing axioms.
problems.description: Various set-theoretic 'union chain condition' problems concern the behavior of unions of chains of structures (posets, boolean algebras, forcing notions) under forcing axioms and in the presence of large cardinals. Specific questions involve the productivity of the countable chain condition, the behavior of Suslin trees, and cardinal invariants of the continuum. Many of these problems are known to be independent of ZFC, and the question is how they behave under standard forcing axioms like MA, PFA, and Martin's Maximum.
problems.abstract: This project investigates set-theoretic chain condition problems for unions in forcing constructions. Our approach combines (1) forcing axioms and their consequences for ccc productivity, (2) Suslin tree combinatorics under large cardinal hypotheses, and (3) cardinal invariants and their interactions with classical combinatorial structures. Progress advances the set-theoretic landscape and has consequences for topology and algebra.

problems.title: Whitehead Problem Generalizations
problems.tagline: Generalizations of the Whitehead problem to larger cardinalities.
problems.summary: Extensions of Shelah's independence result for Whitehead groups in higher cardinalities.
problems.description: The Whitehead problem asks: is every abelian group A with Ext¹(A, ℤ) = 0 free? Shelah (1974) proved that this statement is independent of ZFC — true in Gödel's L (constructible universe) and false in forcing extensions. Generalizations of the Whitehead problem to larger cardinalities and to other categories (modules over specific rings, topological groups) present a rich landscape with many open questions, some decidable in ZFC and others genuinely independent.
problems.abstract: This project investigates Whitehead-type problems beyond the countable case. Our approach combines (1) uncountable combinatorial methods via stationary sets, (2) forcing constructions to separate ZFC-true cases from independent ones, and (3) categorical generalizations involving Ext-groups for rings beyond ℤ. Progress clarifies the interaction between set theory and homological algebra.

problems.title: P vs NP
problems.tagline: Are problems verifiable in polynomial time also solvable in polynomial time?
problems.summary: Does P = NP, the central problem of theoretical computer science?
problems.description: The P vs NP problem asks whether every problem whose solution can be verified in polynomial time can also be solved in polynomial time. It is the central open problem of theoretical computer science and one of the seven Clay Millennium Problems with a $1M prize. Known barriers — relativization (Baker-Gill-Solovay 1975), natural proofs (Razborov-Rudich 1997), and algebrization (Aaronson-Wigderson 2008) — rule out essentially all known proof techniques. Resolution almost certainly requires a fundamentally new method. Nearly all researchers believe P ≠ NP, but the problem is widely regarded as at least decades away.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the P vs NP question, acknowledging that known barriers (relativization, natural proofs, algebrization) rule out standard techniques. Our approach does not promise a full resolution but explores (1) circuit lower bound techniques that escape the natural proofs barrier via explicit non-random gap properties, (2) connections to arithmetic complexity (Valiant's VP ≠ VNP) where more progress seems plausible, and (3) new algebraic methods via the Geometric Complexity Theory program. While a full resolution is generational, intermediate progress has high value.

problems.title: Navier-Stokes Existence and Smoothness
problems.tagline: Do smooth 3D Navier-Stokes solutions exist globally for all smooth initial data?
problems.summary: Clay Millennium Problem: global existence and smoothness of 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes.
problems.description: The Navier-Stokes equations describe viscous incompressible fluid flow. A Clay Millennium Prize is offered for either proving that smooth initial data always yields globally smooth solutions in 3D, or exhibiting a finite-time blow-up. Leray's 1934 construction of weak solutions is classical; uniqueness of weak solutions is open (recent non-uniqueness examples exist for rougher solutions). Partial regularity results (Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg) bound the singular set but do not rule out blow-up. Turbulence itself is not rigorously understood mathematically, and fundamentally new ideas may be required.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Navier-Stokes global regularity problem. Our approach acknowledges that full resolution is a generational challenge and focuses on (1) conditional regularity and improved critical norms beyond Leray-Hopf, (2) turbulence models where dimensional analysis meets rigorous PDE estimates, and (3) numerical evidence with rigorous certification for blow-up versus regularity. Even partial progress on critical quantities has value in PDE theory and mathematical physics.

problems.title: Hodge Conjecture
problems.tagline: Hodge classes on projective varieties are rational combinations of algebraic cycles.
problems.summary: Clay Millennium Problem: rational Hodge classes are combinations of classes of algebraic subvarieties.
problems.description: The Hodge conjecture asserts that for a smooth projective complex algebraic variety X, every rational (p,p)-class in the Hodge decomposition is a rational linear combination of cohomology classes of algebraic subvarieties. It is one of the Clay Millennium Problems. Known cases include (1,1)-classes (Lefschetz theorem) and many specific situations. The general conjecture remains open and is believed to require major progress in motivic theory. Counterexamples to integral versions exist (Atiyah-Hirzebruch, Totaro), but the rational version is open.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Hodge conjecture. Our approach combines (1) motivic-cohomology and absolute Hodge class methods, (2) specific classes of varieties where new techniques might apply (hypersurfaces, abelian varieties of complex multiplication), and (3) connections to the Beilinson-Bloch conjectures and regulator maps. Full resolution likely requires a new framework for motivic cohomology, but intermediate progress is valuable.

problems.title: Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
problems.tagline: The rank of an elliptic curve equals the order of vanishing of its L-function at s = 1.
problems.summary: Clay Millennium Problem: rank of E(ℚ) = ord_{s=1} L(E, s) with leading coefficient formula.
problems.description: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) conjecture relates arithmetic invariants of an elliptic curve E over ℚ (rank, Tate-Shafarevich group, regulator, torsion) to the analytic behavior of its L-function L(E, s) at the central point s = 1. Specifically, ord_{s=1} L(E, s) = rank E(ℚ), and the leading Taylor coefficient has a specific form involving #Ш(E), heights, etc. It is one of the Clay Millennium Problems. Known for analytic rank ≤ 1 (Gross-Zagier, Kolyvagin). Higher rank is completely open. Connected to ASRP research via L-functions and arithmetic geometry.
problems.abstract: This project investigates BSD for elliptic curves of analytic rank ≥ 2 — the main open case. Our approach combines (1) Euler system constructions beyond Kolyvagin, (2) p-adic L-function methods and Iwasawa theory extensions, and (3) ASRP's BC-system framework for L-function zero analysis. While a full proof is a generational challenge, intermediate progress on specific families (e.g., curves over ℚ(√5)) and sharper rank bounds is valuable.

problems.title: Yang-Mills Existence and Mass Gap
problems.tagline: Mathematical construction of quantum Yang-Mills theory with positive mass gap.
problems.summary: Clay Millennium Problem: construct 4D Yang-Mills QFT rigorously and prove positive mass gap.
problems.description: The Yang-Mills existence and mass gap problem asks for a rigorous mathematical construction of 4D quantum Yang-Mills theory and a proof that its spectrum has a positive mass gap (lowest excitation above the vacuum has positive energy). This is one of the Clay Millennium Problems. Lattice gauge theory provides strong numerical evidence but is not a mathematical proof. Constructive QFT has made progress in lower dimensions but 4D remains elusive. Resolution would simultaneously construct a rigorous example of interacting 4D QFT and explain quark confinement.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the Yang-Mills mass gap problem. Our approach acknowledges the problem's foundational difficulty and focuses on (1) lattice gauge theory with continuum limits via renormalization group methods, (2) Osterwalder-Schrader axioms and their verification for specific gauge theories, and (3) connections to constructive QFT in lower dimensions. Progress has consequences for mathematical physics, particle physics, and rigorous foundations of quantum field theory.

problems.title: Langlands Program (Full)
problems.tagline: Automorphic representations correspond to Galois representations.
problems.summary: A grand unification of number theory, representation theory, and harmonic analysis.
problems.description: The Langlands program is a vast web of conjectures connecting number theory, automorphic forms, representation theory, and harmonic analysis. Key conjectures include: the Langlands reciprocity (Galois representations correspond to automorphic representations), functoriality (L-functions transform predictably under representation-theoretic operations), and the local-global principle. Partial progress includes the Taylor-Wiles patching method (proving Fermat's Last Theorem and modularity), Laumon-Ngô-Lafforgue (function field Langlands), and Harris-Taylor (local Langlands for GL_n). The full program will require 50-100 years of work. Connected to ASRP via BC system and automorphic forms.
problems.abstract: This project engages specific cases within the Langlands program. Our approach combines (1) automorphic input for ASRP's BC-system research, (2) local-global Langlands compatibility in specific cases, and (3) Gan-Gross-Prasad and arithmetic theta lifts for L-function special values. Progress connects ASRP's internal research program with the broader Langlands framework, and any specific case settled is a contribution to the grand unified picture.

problems.title: Riemann Hypothesis
problems.tagline: All non-trivial zeros of ζ(s) have real part 1/2.
problems.summary: Clay Millennium Problem: the queen of mathematics.
problems.description: The Riemann Hypothesis (1859) asserts that all non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function ζ(s) = Σ n^(-s) lie on the critical line Re(s) = 1/2. It is the most famous unsolved problem in mathematics, one of the Clay Millennium Problems, and has vast consequences throughout analytic number theory. Known: infinitely many zeros on the critical line (Hardy), at least 40% of zeros on the critical line (Conrey), zero-free regions approaching the critical line (via de la Vallée Poussin and improvements). ASRP's primary mission targets RH via the BC system on ℚ(√5) and related operator-algebraic approaches.
problems.abstract: This project is ASRP's primary research mission: the Riemann Hypothesis via the BC-system approach over ℚ(√5). Our agenda combines (1) spectral analysis of BC-system operators and connection to ζ-zeros via KMS states, (2) refined moment estimates for ζ on the critical line, and (3) the Golden Chain framework linking φ → ℚ(√5) → ζ → α. The work engages multiple facets of the RH problem simultaneously and leverages ASRP's internal research program to attack the central open problem in mathematics.

problems.title: Mathematical Foundations of Quantum Gravity
problems.tagline: A mathematically rigorous theory unifying quantum mechanics and general relativity.
problems.summary: Mathematical foundations for unifying QM and GR remain undeveloped.
problems.description: A mathematically rigorous framework unifying quantum mechanics and general relativity is absent from the literature. String theory, loop quantum gravity, causal dynamical triangulations, and asymptotic safety offer competing approaches but none has a complete mathematical foundation. The lack of experimental guidance (quantum gravity effects at Planck scale are unreachable) further complicates progress. Even the fundamental question of what mathematical structures quantum gravity should employ is debated. ASRP's BC-system → physics bridge offers one speculative avenue.
problems.abstract: This project investigates mathematical foundations for quantum gravity. Our approach combines (1) categorical and higher-algebraic frameworks (TQFT, factorization algebras) suited for combining QM and GR, (2) connections to arithmetic gravity via ASRP's BC-system → physics bridge, and (3) non-commutative geometry approaches following Connes and Marcolli. The project acknowledges the generational scale of the challenge and aims to develop specific mathematical structures that might underlie a future unified theory.

problems.title: Continuum Hypothesis Resolution
problems.tagline: Is there a set of intermediate cardinality between ℕ and ℝ?
problems.summary: Find the right axiom framework to decide CH beyond ZFC.
problems.description: The Continuum Hypothesis (CH) asserts that there is no set with cardinality strictly between that of the integers and the real numbers. Gödel (1940) showed CH is consistent with ZFC; Cohen (1963) showed ¬CH is consistent with ZFC. Thus CH is independent of ZFC. The question now is not 'is CH true?' but 'what additional axioms should be added to ZFC to resolve CH?' Woodin's Ultimate-L program suggests that large cardinal axioms might settle CH in a natural way. This is a foundational question about the nature of mathematical truth.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the axiomatic framework for resolving CH. Our approach combines (1) Woodin's Ultimate-L program and its consequences for cardinal arithmetic, (2) forcing axioms (PFA, MM) and their implications, and (3) philosophical analysis of what it means to 'resolve' an independent question. While a purely mathematical resolution may be impossible, progress in identifying the best axioms has foundational importance.

problems.title: Large Cardinal Hierarchy Completion
problems.tagline: Extend the large cardinal hierarchy to its ultimate boundary.
problems.summary: Understand the structure of all possible large cardinal axioms.
problems.description: Large cardinal axioms extend ZFC by postulating the existence of very large infinite sets (inaccessible, Mahlo, measurable, supercompact, extendible, Vopěnka, Reinhardt). Kunen showed Reinhardt cardinals are inconsistent with AC. Woodin's Ultimate-L program seeks a canonical inner model for the largest conceivable large cardinals. Many open questions remain: which large cardinals are consistent with ZFC? Can the hierarchy be completed coherently? These are foundational questions about the landscape of mathematical truth and the limits of the axiomatic method.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the completion of the large cardinal hierarchy. Our approach combines (1) Woodin's Ultimate-L program and extender models, (2) consistency strength calibration via forcing and inner model theory, and (3) analysis of potential new large cardinals beyond current classifications. Progress has foundational implications for set theory and the axiomatic landscape of mathematics.

problems.title: Finite Model Theory vs Classical
problems.tagline: Structural gaps between finite and classical model theory.
problems.summary: Bridging finite model theory and classical first-order model theory.
problems.description: Finite model theory (the study of first-order logic restricted to finite structures) differs fundamentally from classical model theory: the compactness theorem fails, completeness fails, and many classical techniques do not apply. This creates deep structural gaps, with direct implications for computational complexity (descriptive complexity theory). Whether finite model theory can be coherently extended to capture the tools of classical model theory, and whether there are canonical ways to move between the two, are open foundational questions.
problems.abstract: This project investigates structural connections between finite and classical model theory. Our approach combines (1) descriptive complexity theory and its connection to classical logic, (2) pseudofinite model theory via ultraproducts, and (3) explicit translation techniques between finite and infinite structures. Progress bridges model theory and theoretical computer science with consequences for complexity theory.

problems.title: Decidability of Diophantine Equations
problems.tagline: Classify Diophantine equations by decidability.
problems.summary: DPRM shows general undecidability; specific cases open.
problems.description: Hilbert's 10th problem asked for an algorithm deciding whether a Diophantine equation has an integer solution. The DPRM theorem (Davis, Putnam, Robinson, Matiyasevich, 1970) proved no such algorithm exists in general. The follow-up question is: which restricted classes of Diophantine equations are decidable? For example, solubility of Diophantine equations over ℚ (rational numbers) rather than ℤ remains open. Similarly, the existence of rational points on curves of genus 1 is open (related to BSD). These are deep questions at the interface of number theory and logic.
problems.abstract: This project investigates decidability of restricted Diophantine problems. Our approach combines (1) Hilbert's 10th over ℚ, particularly using elliptic curve structure, (2) decidability for Diophantine sets defined via automorphic forms, and (3) algorithmic advances in computing rational points on curves. Progress clarifies the boundary between decidable and undecidable Diophantine problems.

problems.title: Is Mathematics Discovered or Invented?
problems.tagline: The ontological status of mathematical objects.
problems.summary: Are mathematical objects discovered (Platonism) or invented (formalism/intuitionism)?
problems.description: Whether mathematical objects exist independently of human minds (Platonism) or are creations of human thought (formalism, intuitionism, constructivism) is a philosophical question with deep connections to mathematical practice. Gödel's incompleteness theorems, the independence of CH, and the 'unreasonable effectiveness of mathematics' (Wigner) complicate any simple answer. This is not a mathematical question in the narrow sense — it cannot be resolved by proof — but it deeply influences how mathematicians conceive of their work. Including it acknowledges the philosophical dimension of foundational questions.
problems.abstract: This project engages the philosophical question of whether mathematics is discovered or invented. Our approach is explicitly philosophical: (1) analysis of Platonist, formalist, and intuitionist positions via their mathematical consequences, (2) case studies of surprising mathematical facts (independence of CH, effectiveness of mathematics in physics) that bear on the question, and (3) connection to ASRP's Golden Chain program as a concrete test case for mathematical 'discoveries'. While no definitive answer is expected, the project clarifies foundational commitments.

problems.title: Consistency of Large Cardinal Axioms
problems.tagline: Can we prove large cardinals are consistent?
problems.summary: Consistency of large cardinals cannot be proved from ZFC (by Gödel).
problems.description: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem implies that the consistency of any sufficiently strong theory (like ZFC + large cardinal axioms) cannot be proved within the theory itself. This raises a fundamental question: how can we justify our confidence in the consistency of large cardinal axioms? The answer appears to be a mix of historical track record, inner model theory, reflection principles, and philosophical commitments. Establishing a coherent methodology for justifying consistency claims is a foundational problem in set theory and philosophy of mathematics.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the consistency justification of large cardinal axioms. Our approach combines (1) inner model theory and its role in establishing relative consistency, (2) reflection principles and their implications for new axioms, and (3) philosophical analysis of what it means to justify a mathematical assertion outside the reach of formal proof. Progress clarifies the foundational landscape.

problems.title: Complete Classification of 4-Manifolds
problems.tagline: A complete classification of smooth 4-manifolds.
problems.summary: 4D smooth topology exhibits unique phenomena blocking classification.
problems.description: Smooth manifolds are classified up to diffeomorphism in dimensions 0, 1, 2, 3 (Perelman) and ≥ 5 (via surgery theory). Dimension 4 is unique: smooth 4-manifolds admit exotic smooth structures (ℝ⁴ has uncountably many), and topological and smooth categories diverge dramatically. A complete classification of 4-manifolds, including exotic structures, seems fundamentally out of reach with current techniques. Gauge theory (Donaldson, Seiberg-Witten) gives powerful invariants but does not classify. New tools may be needed.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the classification problem for smooth 4-manifolds. Our approach combines (1) refinement of Seiberg-Witten and Heegaard-Floer invariants, (2) Khovanov-type homology in dimension 4, and (3) case-by-case analysis of specific 4-manifold families. Full classification is likely a generational challenge, but new invariants and structural theorems advance the field significantly.

problems.title: Universal Algebraic Geometry (Grothendieck's Motives)
problems.tagline: Grothendieck's dream of motives: a universal cohomology theory.
problems.summary: Construct the abelian category of mixed motives with all predicted properties.
problems.description: Grothendieck's theory of motives envisions a universal cohomology theory unifying all other cohomology theories (singular, étale, de Rham, crystalline, etc.) into a single 'motivic' framework. Despite decades of work, a complete construction of the abelian category of mixed motives with all predicted properties (triangulated category with t-structure, realization functors, standard conjectures) remains elusive. Voevodsky's derived category of motives is the closest approach but lacks the full expected structure. This is a foundational dream at the heart of algebraic geometry.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the construction of Grothendieck's mixed motives. Our approach combines (1) Voevodsky's triangulated category of motives and the t-structure problem, (2) the standard conjectures on algebraic cycles, and (3) connections to L-function special values via Beilinson's conjectures. Full realization of the dream is generational, but progress on specific facets has cascading consequences throughout algebraic geometry.

problems.title: Unification of Algebra, Geometry, and Analysis
problems.tagline: A unified framework for the three pillars of mathematics.
problems.summary: The deepest meta-mathematical question: is a unification possible?
problems.description: The three classical divisions of mathematics — algebra, geometry, and analysis — have been increasingly unified throughout the 20th and 21st centuries via concepts like algebraic geometry, differential geometry, complex analytic geometry, the Langlands program, and noncommutative geometry. Yet a single unifying framework remains elusive. Whether such unification is possible, or whether the trilogy is irreducibly distinct, is a deep meta-mathematical question. The Langlands program is one serious attempt at partial unification.
problems.abstract: This project investigates the unification of algebra, geometry, and analysis — the deepest meta-mathematical question. Our approach combines (1) the Langlands program as one unification attempt, (2) noncommutative geometry as another (linking operator algebras, number theory, and differential geometry), and (3) higher category theory as a potentially universal language. While the ultimate unification remains speculative, progress on specific bridges between the three pillars is concrete and valuable.
